content: Let me start by reading the blueprint-reviewer instructions and project context, then systematically audit the blueprint files.
I'll read the blueprint-reviewer instructions and project context first, then audit the blueprint files.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
115	---
116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
144	
145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
146	
147	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
148	
149	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
150	
151	## Always read everything
152	
153	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
154	
155	## Unstarted-phase proposals
156	
157	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
158	
159	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
160	
161	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
162	
163	The proposal answers:
164	
165	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
166	
167	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
168	
169	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
170	
171	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
172	
173	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
174	
175	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
176	
177	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
178	
179	```
180	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
181	
182	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
183	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
184	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
185	
186	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
187	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
188	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
189	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
190	...
191	
192	**`\uses` skeleton**:
193	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
194	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
195	
196	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
197	
198	**References for writer**:
199	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
200	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
201	
202	**Subphase choices exposed**:
203	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
204	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
205	```
206	
207	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
208	
209	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
210	
211	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
212

214	
215	```markdown

219	<slug>

222	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
223	
224	## Routes
225	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
226	
227	## References
228	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
229	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
230	
231	## Focus areas (optional)
232	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
233	
234	## Known issues

236	```

238	## What you do
239	
240	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
241	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
242	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
243	   - Read the entire chapter.
244	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
245	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
246	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
247	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
248	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
249	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
250	7. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
251	8. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
252	
253	You may also use:
254	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
255	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
256	
257	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.

259	## Report format
260	
261	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
262	
263	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
264	
265	Concretely:
266	
267	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
268	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
269	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
270	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
271	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
272	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
273	
274	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.

Unstarted-phase proposals do not block existing provers — they trigger blueprint-writer dispatch for future prover work. Do not under-classify them as "informational"; the project pays in deferred parallelism every iter a phase sits without a blueprint.

3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean.ref`): the nine
6	protected declarations headed by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — an Albanese/Jacobian object uniform over the `k`-rational
8	pointing of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C/k` (`[Field k]` only; no `C(k)≠∅`,
9	no `CharZero`). `J := Pic⁰_{C/k}` is built unconditionally; only `isAlbaneseFor` is quantified over
10	the pointing. End-state: zero inline `sorry` in the dependency cone of each protected decl, 0
11	project axioms, kernel-only axioms. Spine: pointed vs. unpointed. Posture **option (c)**: forward
12	the Route-A Picard substrate while Riemann–Roch stays frozen by the permanent USER Route-C pause.

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
79	    thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
80	
81	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
82	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
83	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
84	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
85	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
86	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
87	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
88	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
89	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
90	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
91	  %  [\dots]
92	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
93	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
94	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
95	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
96	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
97	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
98	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
99	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
100	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
101	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
102	  \[
103	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
104	  \]
105	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
106	  \[
107	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
108	  \]
109	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
110	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
111	  \[
112	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
113	  \]
114	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
115	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
116	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
117	\end{lemma}

164	  closure); both are themselves deferred targets.
165	
166	  \emph{Step 1 (abelian group on the carrier).} On isomorphism classes of the
167	  carrier \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T)\), addition is the descent of the
168	  scheme-level tensor product of line bundles,
169	  \[
170	    [L] + [L'] \;:=\; [\, L \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} L' \,],
171	  \]
172	  where \(L \otimes L'\) is the locally-trivial descent of the scheme-level
173	  tensor product (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}); the sum lands again in
174	  the carrier because the tensor product of two locally-trivial modules is
175	  locally trivial (\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}). The zero
176	  element is the class \([\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\) of the structure sheaf,
177	  the monoidal unit, which is locally trivial. Associativity, commutativity,
178	  and left/right unitality of \(+\) descend from the scheme-level coherence
179	  isomorphisms --- the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the
180	  braiding (\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}), and the unitors
181	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) --- exactly as for the absolute Picard group
182	  on tensor-invertible isomorphism classes
183	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The additive inverse is
184	  \[
185	    -[L] \;:=\; [\, L^{\mathrm{inv}} \,],
186	  \]
187	  where \(L^{\mathrm{inv}}\) is the witness supplied by
188	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}: it returns a \emph{locally-trivial}
189	  module together with an isomorphism
190	  \(L \otimes L^{\mathrm{inv}} \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\), so the inverse
191	  stays inside the loc-triv carrier and the group is closed under negation.
192	
193	  \emph{Step 2 (the pullback homomorphism \(\pi_T^*\)).} Pullback along the
194	  projection \(\pi_T\) gives an additive map
195	  \[
196	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T),
197	  \]
198	  whose underlying assignment of line bundles is
199	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}. It is a homomorphism of abelian groups:
200	  it sends the unit to the unit, since
201	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{O}_T \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) by the unconditional
202	  pullback--unit isomorphism (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}); and it is additive,
203	  since pullback commutes with tensor product on locally-trivial modules,
204	  \[
205	    \pi_T^*(N \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} N') \;\cong\;
206	      \pi_T^* N \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^* N',
207	  \]
208	  by the locally-trivial comparison isomorphism
209	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} specialised to the projection
210	  \(\pi_T\) on the \(\mathrm{OnProduct}\) carrier. This is the exact analogue,
211	  for the geometric pullback, of the additive monoid homomorphism on Picard
212	  groups induced by a ring map respecting tensor product, and is modelled on it
213	  field-for-field. Write \(H_T := \mathrm{im}(\pi_T^*)\) for its image, a
214	  subgroup of the carrier's isomorphism-class group.
215	
216	  \emph{Step 3 (setoid reconciliation).} The set-valued quotient of
217	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is taken modulo the
218	  isomorphism-class equivalence that declares two line bundles related when
219	  their classes differ by an element of \(H_T\). This relation coincides with
220	  the left-coset relation of the subgroup \(H_T\) inside the abelian group of
221	  Steps~1--2: both encode
222	  \[
223	    L \sim L' \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
224	      [\, L^{\mathrm{inv}} \otimes L' \,] \in H_T .
225	  \]
226	  Establishing this equivalence of relations is a concrete isomorphism-class
227	  argument over the carrier; it consumes the tensor inverse (Step~1) and the
228	  group law, but not the computational content of the comparison isomorphism.
229	
230	  \emph{Step 4 (transport).} By Step~3 the quotient set underlying
231	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is in canonical bijection with the group quotient
232	  \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T) / H_T\), which carries the canonical
233	  abelian-group structure of a quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup.
234	  Transporting this structure along the bijection of Step~3 endows
235	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) with its abelian-group structure, under which the
236	  canonical surjection
237	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a
238	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
239	\end{proof}

241	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
242	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
243	
244	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
245	group-valued presheaf.

247	\begin{definition}\leanok
248	[Relative Picard presheaf]

268	  \[

276	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
277	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
278	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.

422	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
423	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
424	
425	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
426	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
427	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
428	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
429	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
430	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
431	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
432	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
433	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.

571	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf\_group\_structure at
572	% b80f227 supplies a zero natural transformation as the Nonempty
573	% witness
574	% (\(\langle 0\rangle : \texttt{Nonempty}\,(\texttt{presheaf}\ C
575	%   \Rightarrow \texttt{etSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\)),
576	% NOT the canonical sheafification unit. The Lean statement type has
577	% been weakened to bare \texttt{Nonempty} existence to admit this
578	% trivial witness; the canonical morphism with its universal property
579	% is the content of \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical} and
580	% is deferred. The closure is gated on the upstream Mathlib
581	% \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure gap. DO NOT promote this
582	% block to \leanok-on-the-canonical-statement via the deterministic
583	% \texttt{sync\_leanok} phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in review
584	% beyond the weak \texttt{Nonempty} content, until the body is
585	% replaced with the canonical sheafification unit and the prose is
586	% upgraded accordingly.
587	\begin{proof}
588	
589	
590	\leanok
591	  The canonical morphism from a presheaf to its associated sheaf in
592	  any Grothendieck topology (Stacks tag 00WI; the canonical
593	  sheafification unit) supplies, in particular, a morphism of
594	  presheaves \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); the
595	  hom-set of presheaf morphisms between these two objects is therefore
596	  nonempty. The full universal property of this canonical morphism
597	  --- and consequently the claim that the abelian-group structure on
598	  the sheafification is uniquely pinned by it --- is the content of
599	  the forward-looking \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
600	  below; see its proof sketch for the canonical formulation.
601	\end{proof}
602	
603	\begin{theorem}[Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)]
604	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
605	
606	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
607	  % Def.~df:Pfs + the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following
608	  % (the \'etale sheaf is characterised as the sheaf associated to the
609	  % relative Picard functor, via the natural map from a presheaf to its
610	  % associated sheaf --- i.e.\ the sheafification unit with its universal
611	  % property)
612	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1328).
613	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
614	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
615	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
616	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
617	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
618	  % topologies  by
619	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
620	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
621	  %  \end{dfn}
622	  %  [\dots]
623	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
624	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
625	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
626	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
627	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
628	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
629	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
630	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
631	  Def.~df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph
632	  immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its
633	  associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is
634	  universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic_{X/S}\) into \'etale
635	  sheaves of abelian groups).}
636	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There
637	  exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
638	  \[
639	    \eta \;\colon\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\;
640	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}
641	  \]
642	  with the following universal property. For every \'etale sheaf
643	  \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and every morphism
644	  of presheaves of abelian groups
645	  \(\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique
646	  morphism of \'etale sheaves of abelian groups
647	  \(\tilde\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that
648	  \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi\). Equivalently, the pair
649	  \(\bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}, \, \eta\bigr)\) is initial among
650	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
651	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
652	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
653	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
654	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
655	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
656	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
657	  \(\eta\).
658	\end{theorem}
659	
660	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
661	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
662	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
663	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
664	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
665	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
666	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
667	\begin{proof}
668	
669	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
670	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
671	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
672	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
673	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
674	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
675	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
676	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
677	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
678	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
679	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
680	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
681	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
682	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
683	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
684	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
685	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
686	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
687	\end{proof}

28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}
87	
88	% NOTE: iter-174 Lane E (file-skeleton) encodes the carrier `OnProduct πC πT`
89	% as `Type (u+1) := sorry` (a TYPE-level scaffold sorry), mirroring the
90	% iter-173 precedent for `Scheme.QcohAlgebra`. Mathlib commit `b80f227`
91	% ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on `Scheme.Modules`; the closest is

127	\section{Project-side IsLocallyTrivial substrate}
128	\label{sec:lbp_isLocallyTrivial_substrate}
129	
130	Mathlib at the pinned commit \texttt{b80f227} ships no
131	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible} predicate (iter-187 \texttt{lean\_local\_search}
132	verdict: empty). The project introduces a stand-in predicate
133	\texttt{Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial} encoding rank-one local
134	trivialisation on an affine cover. The subtype
135	\texttt{\{ M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M \}}
136	gives the carrier of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.
137	
138	\begin{definition}
139	
140	\leanok
141	[Local triviality of a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
142	  \label{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}
144	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
145	  (locally trivial $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules), specialised to rank one.}
146	  An \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\) on a scheme \(X\) is
147	  \emph{locally trivial of rank one} if for every point \(x \in X\) there
148	  exists an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an iso
149	  \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the structure sheaf restricted to
150	  \(U\) viewed as the unit object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)). Formally:
151	  \[
152	    \forall\, x : X, \exists\, U : X.\mathrm{Opens},
153	      x \in U \,\wedge\, \mathtt{IsAffineOpen}\, U \,\wedge\,
154	      \mathtt{Nonempty}\, (\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{restrict}\, U.\iota \cong
155	        \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\, \_).
156	  \]
157	\end{definition}
158	
159	\begin{lemma}
160	
161	\leanok
162	[Pullback preserves local triviality (Stacks 01HH)]
163	  \label{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}
165	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
166	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
167	  (pullback of a locally trivial module is locally trivial).}
168	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
169	  \(\mathcal{M} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be locally trivial of rank one
170	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is locally
171	  trivial of rank one.
172	\end{lemma}
173	\begin{proof}
174	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
175	  \leanok
176	  Given \(y : Y\), let \(x = f(y) \in X\). By the local triviality of
177	  \(\mathcal{M}\) at \(x\), there is an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an
178	  iso \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The preimage \(f^{-1}U\)
179	  is an open neighbourhood of \(y\), and any sufficiently small affine
180	  open \(V \subseteq f^{-1}U\) containing \(y\) (exists by
181	  \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}) gives the chart. The
182	  required iso
183	  \((f^*\mathcal{M})|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) follows from the chain:
184	  \texttt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback} (identifying \(\mathrm{restrict}\, V.\iota\)
185	  with \(\mathrm{pullback}\, V.\iota\)), \texttt{pullbackComp} (composing
186	  pullback functors along \(V.\iota \circ f = (g : V \to U) \circ U.\iota\)),
187	  and \texttt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit} (pullback of unit is unit; uses
188	  \(\mathtt{F.Final}\) instance for the underlying continuous functor of an
189	  open immersion).
190	
191	  % NOTE (iter-188 plan-phase): Body lands with 1 narrow named typed sorry
192	  % on the residual chart-iso construction; the affine-chart existence step
193	  % is closed axiom-clean. Closure target iter-188 prover, ~30-50 LOC.
194	\end{proof}

196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
206	
207	\begin{definition}\leanok
208	[Pullback along the projection]
209	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
213	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
214	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
216	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
217	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
218	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
219	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
220	  %  [\dots]
221	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
222	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
223	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
224	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
225	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
226	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
227	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
228	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
229	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
230	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
231	  in this chapter)
232	  \[
233	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
234	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
235	  \]
236	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
237	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
238	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
239	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
240	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
241	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
242	  the relative Picard presheaf.
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as
246	% the direct application `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`,
247	% axiom-clean modulo the carrier-level simplification on `OnProduct`. The
248	% invertibility-preservation step (Stacks 01HH: pullback of invertible is
249	% invertible) is deferred to iter-187+ once `OnProduct` carries an
250	% invertibility witness.
251	
252	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
253	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
261	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
262	  % includes composition)
263	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
266	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
267	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
268	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
270	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
271	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
272	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
273	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
274	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
275	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
276	  canonical projections, and write
277	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
278	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
279	  pullback maps
280	  \[
281	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
282	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
283	  \]
284	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
285	  \[
286	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
287	  \]
288	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
289	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
290	\end{lemma}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
293	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
294	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
295	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
296	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  
300	\leanok
301	  The square
302	  \[
303	    \begin{array}{ccc}
304	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
305	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
306	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
307	    \end{array}
308	  \]
309	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
310	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
312	  there is a canonical isomorphism
313	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
314	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
315	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
316	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
317	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
318	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
319	  set-valued maps stated above.
320	\end{proof}
321	
322	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
323	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
324	
325	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
326	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
327	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
331	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
335	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
336	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
338	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
339	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
340	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
341	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
342	  % topologies  by
343	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
344	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
345	  %  \end{dfn}"
346	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
347	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
348	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
349	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
350	  \[
351	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
352	  \]
353	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
354	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
355	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
356	  a subgroup). Define
357	  \[
358	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
359	  \]
360	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
361	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
362	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
364	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
365	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as a
370	% `Setoid` on `OnProduct = (pullback πC πT).Modules` whose relation is
371	% `r L L' := Nonempty (L ≅ L')` (iso-class equivalence; axiom-clean). This
372	% identifies isomorphic modules (giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)`
373	% once invertibility is imposed in iter-187+) but does NOT yet quotient by
374	% the pullback subgroup `pi_T^* Pic(T)`. The full quotient requires (a) a
375	% tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib ships
376	% `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
377	% `Scheme.Modules` directly at b80f227), and (b) an inverse for invertible
378	% sheaves (depends on the iter-187+ `IsInvertible` predicate). Iter-187+
379	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
380	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.
381	
382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
383	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
384	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
385	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
386	% not a translation of an external argument.
387	\begin{proof}
388	  
389	  
390	\leanok
391	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
392	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
393	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
394	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
395	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
396	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
397	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
398	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
399	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
400	\end{proof}
401	
402	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
403	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
404	
405	\begin{theorem}\leanok
406	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
407	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
409	  
410	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
411	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
412	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
415	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
416	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
417	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
418	  %  [\dots]
419	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
422	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
423	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
424	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
425	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
426	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
427	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
428	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
429	  \[
430	    \begin{array}{ccc}
431	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
432	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
433	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
434	    \end{array}
435	  \]
436	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
437	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
438	  unique set map
439	  \[
440	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
441	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
442	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
443	  \]
444	  through the quotients of
445	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
446	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
447	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
448	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
449	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
450	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
454	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
455	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
456	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  
460	\leanok
461	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
462	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
463	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
464	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
465	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
466	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
467	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
468	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
469	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
470	  universal property of the quotient set
471	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
472	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
473	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
474	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
475	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
476	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
477	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
478	\end{proof}
