300	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
301	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
302	
303	\begin{definition}
304	
305	
306	\leanok
307	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
308	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
310	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
311	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
312	  \[
313	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
314	  \]
315	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
316	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
317	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
318	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
319	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
320	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
321	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
322	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
323	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
324	  canonical compatibility
325	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
326	     \;\cong\;
327	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
328	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
329	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
330	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
331	
332	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
333	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
334	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
335	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
336	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
337	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
338	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
339	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
340	\end{definition}
341	
342	\begin{lemma}
343	
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
347	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
351	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
353	  \[
354	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
357	  \]
358	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
359	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
360	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
361	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
362	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
363	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
364	  \[
365	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
366	    \qquad
367	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
368	  \]
369	  the associator
370	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
371	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
372	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
373	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
374	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
379	  \leanok
380	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
381	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
382	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
383	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
384	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
385	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
386	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
387	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
388	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
389	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
390	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
391	\end{proof}
392	
393	\begin{remark}
394	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
395	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
396	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
397	        lem:whisker_of_W}
398	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
399	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
400	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
401	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
402	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
403	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
404	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
405	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
406	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
407	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
408	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
409	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
410	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
411	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
412	
413	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
414	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
415	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
416	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
417	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
418	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
419	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
420	  fixed-base presheaves in
421	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
422	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
423	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
424	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
425	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
426	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
427	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
428	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
429	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
430	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
431	
432	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
433	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
434	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
435	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
436	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
437	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
438	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
439	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
440	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
441	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
442	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
443	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
444	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
445	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
446	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
447	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
448	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
449	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
450	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
451	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
452	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
453	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
454	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
455	\end{remark}
456	
457	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
458	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
459	
460	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
461	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
462	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
463	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
464	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
465	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
466	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
467	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
469	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
470	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
471	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
472	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
473	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
474	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
475	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
476	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
477	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
478	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
479	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
480	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
481	to the off-path route~(e).
482

484

59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
73	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	\(J\).
77	
78	The universal \(T\)-points of \(J\) are degree-\(0\) line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T\(, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from \)T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A\( corresponding to a pointed morphism \)\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) defines a
87	regular morphism \(\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\), the
88	birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\) inverts to a
89	rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism \(\psi\).
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	
97	\leanok
98	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
99	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
101	  
102	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
103	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
104	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
105	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
106	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
107	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
108	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
109	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
110	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
111	
112	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
113	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
114	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
115	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
116	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
117	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
118	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
119	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
120	\end{theorem}
121	
122	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
123	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
124	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
125	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
126	then gives the proof.
127	
128	\section{The Abel--Jacobi morphism at a marked point}
129	\label{sec:albaneseup_abel_jacobi}
130	
131	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and the elementary regularity of
132	the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\) at the basepoint \(P_0\). It is the
133	content gated on A.3 (existence of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a representable group
134	scheme).
135	
136	\begin{lemma}\leanok
137	[Abel--Jacobi morphism at a basepoint]
138	  \label{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}
140	  
141	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, the canonical map
142	  % \(f^P\colon C\to J\) (introduction to Proposition~6.1), doc p.~104
143	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
145	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
146	  %  \(f^P\colon C\to J\) has the following universal property: for any map
147	  %  \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending \(P\)
148	  %  to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such that
149	  %  \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
150	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, Summary 6.11, doc p.~107
151	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 113).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
153	  % "SUMMARY 6.11.  Between \((C,P)\) and itself, there is a divisorial
154	  %  correspondence \(\mathcal L^P = \mathcal L(\Delta-\{P\}\times C-C\times\{P\})\).
155	  %    Between \((C,P)\) and \(J\) there is the divisorial correspondence
156	  %  \(\mathcal M^P\); for any divisorial correspondence \(\mathcal L\) between
157	  %  \((C,P)\) and a pointed \(k\)-scheme \((T,t)\), there is a unique morphism
158	  %  of pointed \(k\)-schemes \(\varphi\colon T\to J\) such that
159	  %  \((1\times \varphi)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L\).  In particular,
160	  %  there is a unique map \(f^P\colon C\to J\) such that
161	  %  \((1\times f^P)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L^P\) and \(f(P)=0\)."
162	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1 and
163	  Summary~6.11, \S III.6.}
164	
165	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\), let
166	  \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\). There is a
167	  unique regular morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
168	  \[
169	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J, \qquad
170	    Q \;\longmapsto\; \bigl[\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\bigr],
171	  \]
172	  sending the basepoint \(P_0\) to the identity element \(\eta_J \in J(\bar k)\)
173	  and characterised on \(T\)-points by the formula
174	  \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\), where
175	  $\mathcal L^{P_0} := \mathcal O_{C \times C}\bigl(\Delta - \{P_0\} \times C -
176	  C \times \{P_0\}\bigr)$ is the rigidified diagonal correspondence on
177	  \(C \times C\) and \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) is the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence
178	  on \(C \times J\).
179	\end{lemma}
180	
181	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (no separate verbatim source; the existence of
182	% \(f^P\) is part of Milne's standing setup at the head of \S III.6 -- ``\(J\)
183	% will be its Jacobian variety'' -- and is the moduli-of-degree-0-line-bundles
184	% interpretation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) packaged into Summary~6.11.)
185	\begin{proof}
186	  
187	  By A.3, \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the moduli scheme of degree-zero line bundles
188	  on \(C\); concretely, for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) its \(T\)-points are
189	  isomorphism classes of line bundles \(\mathcal L\) on \(C \times_{\bar k} T\)
190	  whose fibre degree at every geometric point of \(T\) is zero, taken modulo line
191	  bundles pulled back from \(T\) (the universal property of the Picard scheme
192	  packaged in \cref{def:pic_scheme}; the identity component imposes the
193	  degree-zero condition).
194	
195	  Apply this to \(T := C\) and the line bundle
196	  \[
197	    \mathcal L^{P_0} \;:=\; \mathcal O_{C \times_{\bar k} C}\bigl(\Delta -
198	      \{P_0\} \times C - C \times \{P_0\}\bigr),
199	  \]
200	  the rigidified diagonal correspondence on \(C \times_{\bar k} C\). Its fibre
201	  over a \(\bar k\)-point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) along the second projection is the
202	  degree-zero line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\) on \(C\), so \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\)
203	  is a relative degree-zero line bundle, hence a \(C\)-point of \(J\). The
204	  representability of \(J\) at \(T = C\) produces the unique morphism
205	  \[
206	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J
207	  \]
208	  such that \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
209	  for the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) on \(C \times J\).
210	
211	  At the basepoint, restriction of \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\) to \(C \times \{P_0\}\)
212	  gives \(\mathcal O_C(P_0 - P_0) \cong \mathcal O_C\), the trivial line bundle,
213	  whose moduli class in \(J(\bar k)\) is the identity element \(\eta_J\). Hence
214	  \(\iota_{P_0}(P_0) = \eta_J\). Uniqueness of \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the moduli
215	  uniqueness clause of representability: any other morphism \(\iota'_{P_0}\)
216	  satisfying \((1 \times \iota'_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
217	  and the pointed condition agrees with \(\iota_{P_0}\) on \(T\)-points for every
218	  \(T\), hence equals \(\iota_{P_0}\) by the Yoneda embedding.
219	\end{proof}
220	
221	\section{The \(g\)-th symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)}
222	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g}
223	
224	The Albanese universal property is built from \(C\) by taking the
225	\(g\)-th \emph{symmetric power} \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) -- the quotient of the
226	\(g\)-fold Cartesian power \(C^g\) by the natural action of the symmetric group
227	\(S_g\). We collect the definition here as the first sub-lemma block.
228	
229	\begin{definition}\leanok
230	[Symmetric power of a curve]
231	  \label{def:symmetric_power_curve}
232	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}
233	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~3.1, \S III.3,
234	  % doc p.~94 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 100).
235	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
236	  % "For any variety \(V\), the symmetric group \(S_r\) on \(r\) letters acts on
237	  %  the product of \(r\) copies \(V^r\) of \(V\) by permuting the factors, and
238	  %  we want to define the \(r\)th symmetric power \(V^{(r)}\) of \(V\) to be the
239	  %  quotient \(S_r \backslash V^r\).  The next proposition demonstrates the
240	  %  existence of \(V^{(r)}\) and lists its main properties.
241	  %    A morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) is said to be {\bf symmetric} if
242	  %  \(\varphi\sigma=\varphi\) for all \(\sigma\) in \(S_r\).
243	  %  PROPOSITION 3.1. Let \(V\) be a variety over \(k\).  Then there exists a
244	  %  variety \(V^{(r)}\) and a symmetric morphism \(\pi\colon V^r\to V^{(r)}\)
245	  %  having the following properties:
246	  %    (a) as a topological space, \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) is the quotient of \(V^r\)
247	  %        by \(S_r\);
248	  %    (b) for any open affine subset \(U\) of \(V\), \(U^{(r)}\) is an open
249	  %        affine subset of \(V^{(r)}\) and
250	  %             $\Gamma(U^{(r)},\mathcal O_{V^{(r)}}) = \Gamma(U^r,
251	  %             \mathcal O_{V^r})^{S_r}$
252	  %        (set of elements fixed by the action of \(S_r\)).
253	  %  The pair \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) has the following universal property: every
254	  %  symmetric \(k\)-morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) factors uniquely
255	  %  through \(\pi\).
256	  %  The map \(\pi\) is finite, surjective, and separable."
257	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~3.1, \S III.3, p.~94.}
258	
259	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over an algebraically closed field
260	  \(\bar k\) and let \(g \ge 1\). The \emph{\(g\)-th symmetric power}
261	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is a \(\bar k\)-scheme equipped with a finite surjective
262	  symmetric morphism \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) characterised by
263	  the following universal property: for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) and every
264	  symmetric \(\bar k\)-morphism \(\varphi \colon C^g \to T\) (i.e.\ a morphism
265	  satisfying \(\varphi \circ \sigma = \varphi\) for all \(\sigma \in S_g\)
266	  acting by permutation of the factors of \(C^g\)) there exists a unique
267	  morphism \(\overline\varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to T\) with
268	  \(\overline\varphi \circ \pi = \varphi\). As a topological space
269	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is the quotient of \(C^g\) by \(S_g\), and for every open
270	  affine \(U \subseteq C\) the open subset $U^{(g)} \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g
271	  C\( is affine with global sections the \)S_g$-invariants
272	  \(\Gamma(U^g, \mathcal O_{C^g})^{S_g}\).
273	\end{definition}
274	
275	\medskip
276	
277	\textbf{Mathlib status.} Mathlib has no formalised \(g\)-th symmetric power of
278	a scheme: only \texttt{Sym}/\texttt{SymmetricPower} for types and modules.
279	The corresponding scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied
280	project-side. The intended Lean construction follows Milne's recipe of
281	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}: on affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq C\)
282	form \(\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}\), then glue via the standard affine
283	patching for an open cover \(C = \bigcup U_i\). This sub-build is a
284	non-trivial dependency of A.4.d that the prover lane must absorb before the
285	main universal property closes; a \texttt{\textbackslash notready} marker is
286	appropriate on the corresponding Lean target until the construction lands.
287	For the proof of Proposition~3.1 itself, Milne refers to Mumford 1970,
288	\S II~\S 7, p.~66 and \S III \S 11, p.~112, for the patching details (the
289	quoted reference at the end of Milne's Proof on \S III.3, p.~94).
290	
291	\section{The morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)}
292	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_av_map}
293	
294	The second sub-lemma is the elementary fact that the symmetric assignment
295	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) from
296	\(C^g\) to \(A\) descends through \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\). The
297	key point is that the addition morphism \(A^g \to A\) is itself
298	\(S_g\)-symmetric because \(A\) is commutative as a group scheme.
299	
300	\begin{lemma}\leanok
301	[Symmetrisation of a curve-to-AV morphism]
302	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}
304	  
305	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
306	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
307	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
308	  % "PROOF.  Consider the map
309	  %     \((P_1,...,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\)
310	  %  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\)."
311	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
312	  \S III.6, p.~104.}
313	
314	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
315	  over \(\bar k\). The composition
316	  \[
317	    \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}
318	    \;\colon\; C^g \;\longrightarrow\; A^g \;\longrightarrow\; A,
319	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\; \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g),
320	  \]
321	  is \(S_g\)-equivariant (where \(S_g\) acts on \(C^g\) by permutation of the
322	  factors and on \(A\) trivially). Consequently, by the universal property of
323	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) (\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}), there is a unique
324	  morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
325	  \[
326	    \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A
327	  \]
328	  such that
329	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ \pi = \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\).
330	\end{lemma}
331	
332	\begin{proof}
333	  
334	  Since \(A\) is a commutative group scheme, the \(g\)-fold addition morphism
335	  \(\mathrm{add}_A \colon A^g \to A\) satisfies $\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau =
336	  \mathrm{add}_A\( for every \)\tau \in S_g\( permuting the factors of \)A^g$
337	  (this is the scheme-theoretic statement that summation in a commutative
338	  monoid is independent of the order of summands; on \(T\)-points it is the
339	  identity \(a_1 + \cdots + a_g = a_{\tau(1)} + \cdots + a_{\tau(g)}\) in the
340	  abelian group \(A(T)\)). The diagonal action of \(\tau\) on $\varphi^{\times
341	  g} \colon C^g \to A^g$ is also given by permutation of factors. Therefore
342	  \[
343	    (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}) \circ \tau
344	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\varphi^{\times g} \circ \tau)
345	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\tau \circ \varphi^{\times g})
346	    \;=\; (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau) \circ \varphi^{\times g}
347	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g},
348	  \]
349	  so the composition \(\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\) is symmetric
350	  in the sense of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}. The universal property
351	  of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the unique factorisation
352	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\).
353	\end{proof}
354	
355	\section{The birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\)}
356	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_to_jacobian}
357	
358	The third sub-lemma is the birational identification of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)
359	with \(J\). After choosing the basepoint \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\), the cycle map
360	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\)
361	defines a morphism \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) which is
362	birational by Milne III~Theorem~5.1(a).
363	
364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
365	[Birational morphism from \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to \(J\)]
366	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}
367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}
368	  
369	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~5.1(a), \S III.5,
370	  % doc p.~101 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 107).
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
372	  % "Let \(f^r\) be the map \(C^r\to J\) sending \((P_1,...,P_r)\) to
373	  %  \(f(P_1)+\cdots+f(P_r)\).  On points, \(f^r\) is the map
374	  %  \((P_1,...,P_r)\mapsto[P_1+\cdots+P_r-rP]\).  Clearly it is symmetric,
375	  %  and so induces a map \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to J\).  We can regard
376	  %  \(f^{(r)}\) as being the map sending an effective divisor \(D\) of degree
377	  %  \(r\) on \(C\) to the linear equivalence class of \(D-rP\).  The fibre of
378	  %  the map \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}(k)\to J(k)\) containing \(D\) can be
379	  %  identified with the space of effective divisors linearly equivalent
380	  %  to \(D\), that is, with the linear system \(|D|\).  The image of \(C^{(r)}\)
381	  %  in \(J\) is a closed subvariety \(W^r\) of \(J\), which can also be written
382	  %  \(W^r=f(C)+\cdots+f(C)\) (\(r\) summands).
383	  %  THEOREM 5.1.  (a) For all \(r\le g\), the morphism
384	  %  \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to W^r\) is birational; in particular,
385	  %  \(f^{(g)}\) is a birational map from \(C^{(g)}\) onto \(J\)."
386	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~5.1(a) and the
387	  preceding paragraph, \S III.5, p.~101.}
388	
389	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\),
390	  let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\),
391	  \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the Abel--Jacobi morphism of
392	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The regular morphism
393	  \(\iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J^g\) composed with the \(g\)-fold
394	  group-law addition \(\mathrm{add}_J \colon J^g \to J\) descends through
395	  \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to a regular morphism
396	  \[
397	    f^{(g)} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; J,
398	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\;
399	      \bigl[\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)\bigr].
400	  \]
401	  The morphism \(f^{(g)}\) is birational: it is dominant (its image is
402	  \(J\), the \(g\)-fold sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) exhausts
403	  \(J\) since \(\dim J = g\) and the iterated sum reaches full dimension), and
404	  its generic fibre is a single \(\bar k\)-point (a degree-\(g\) effective
405	  divisor \(D\) on \(C\) with \(h^0(D) = 1\) is the unique element of its linear
406	  system \(|D|\); such divisors form a dense open subset of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
407	  C\( -- a consequence of \)\dim |D| = 0\( generically on a curve of genus \)g$
408	  by Riemann--Roch). Equivalently, \(f^{(g)}\) is a birational morphism in
409	  the sense that there exist dense opens
410	  \(U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C\), \(V \subseteq J\) such that
411	  \(f^{(g)}|_U \colon U \to V\) is an isomorphism.
412	\end{lemma}
413	
414	% NOTE (iter-199): the birationality proof below derives the generic-fibre
415	% identification from Riemann--Roch at \(\deg D = g\) (specifically
416	% \(h^0(D) - h^1(D) = 1\) generically). Riemann--Roch is the Route C
417	% chain (\texttt{RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex} et al.), currently PAUSED
418	% per USER 2026-05-28 standing directive. This lemma is therefore
419	% gated on Route C re-engagement (or on a parallel construction that
420	% sidesteps RR). Flagged here so a future prover does not attempt to
421	% formalize the birationality argument until the RR substrate is
422	% available.
423	
424	\begin{proof}
425	
426	  Symmetry of \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g}\) is the
427	  specialisation of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} to the abelian
428	  variety \(A := J\) and the morphism \(\varphi := \iota_{P_0}\); the universal
429	  property of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the regular morphism \(f^{(g)}\).
430	  The closed-form description on \(\bar k\)-points,
431	  \(f^{(g)}(P_1, \ldots, P_g) = [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\),
432	  follows because \(\iota_{P_0}(P_i) = [\mathcal O_C(P_i - P_0)]\) by
433	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} and the group law on \(J\) is the
434	  tensor-product of degree-zero line bundles.
435	
436	  We verify birationality. The image of \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is the iterated
437	  sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) summands), which is
438	  closed (it is the image of the proper morphism
439	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) being proper because \(C^g\)
440	  is proper and \(\pi\) is finite surjective). By the dimension count
441	  \(\dim \mathrm{Sym}^g C = g = \dim J\) (Riemann--Roch and the standing
442	  hypothesis \(g > 0\)), the image \(W^g \subseteq J\) has dimension at most
443	  \(g\), and equality forces \(W^g = J\) since \(J\) is irreducible. Hence
444	  \(f^{(g)}\) is surjective with \(\dim\)-equal source and target.
445	
446	  For the generic-fibre identification, Riemann--Roch
447	  \(h^0(D) - h^1(D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) at \(\deg D = g\) gives $h^0(D) - h^1(D)
448	  = 1\(, so for a general \)D \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\( with \)h^1(D) =
449	  0\( (the locus where \)h^1$ jumps is closed of positive codimension on a
450	  smooth curve, by upper semicontinuity of cohomology), we have $h^0(D) =
451	  1\( and the linear system \)|D|\( consists of \)D$ alone. Therefore the
452	  fibre of \(f^{(g)}\) over the generic point of \(J\) contains a single point
453	  \(D\), i.e.\ \(f^{(g)}\) is birational onto \(J\).
454	
455	  By \cref{def:pic_scheme} the underlying \(\bar k\)-scheme \(J\) is the
456	  Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) obtained at A.3, so the moduli-theoretic
457	  reading of \(f^{(g)}\) (effective divisor class \(\mapsto\) degree-zero
458	  line bundle class via \(D \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(D - g P_0)]\)) is the
459	  same as the Yoneda image of the \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)-point
460	  $[\mathcal O_{C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g C}(\mathcal D_g - g\{P_0\} \times
461	  \mathrm{Sym}^g C)]\(, where \)\mathcal D_g \subseteq C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g
462	  C\( is the universal effective Cartier divisor of degree \)g\( on \)C$. We
463	  do not need this Yoneda repackaging for the proof of the Albanese UP --
464	  the symmetric-power route uses only the morphism \(f^{(g)}\) and its
465	  birationality.
466	\end{proof}
467	
468	\section{Descent through the birational quotient \(\sigma\)}
469	\label{sec:albaneseup_descent}
470	
471	The final auxiliary lemma packages the two-step descent that promotes a
472	morphism on \(C^g\) (after passing through \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) via
473	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and inverting \(f^{(g)}\) via
474	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}) to a regular morphism on
475	\(J\). The output is a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), which is then
476	upgraded to a regular morphism via Milne's Theorem~3.2
477	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}). This is the unique step of the
478	proof that consumes A.4.c.
479	
480	\begin{lemma}\leanok
481	[Descent of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\)]
482	  \label{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}
483	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}
484	  
485	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
486	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
487	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
488	  % "It therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
489	  %  shows to be a regular map."
490	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
491	  \S III.6, p.~104 (the sentence ``which (I~3.2) shows to be a regular
492	  map'').}
493	
494	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
495	  over \(\bar k\). Let \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)
496	  be the symmetrised morphism of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and
497	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) the birational morphism of
498	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}. Then there is a unique
499	  regular morphism
500	  \[
501	    \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A
502	  \]
503	  such that \(\psi \circ f^{(g)} = \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi\) on the dense open
504	  on which \(f^{(g)}\) is an isomorphism (equivalently, \(\psi\) is the unique
505	  regular morphism extending the rational map
506	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1} \colon J \dashrightarrow
507	  A$).
508	\end{lemma}
509	
510	\begin{proof}
511	  
512	  By \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, there exist dense opens
513	  \(U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) and \(V \subseteq J\) with $f^{(g)}|_U \colon
514	  U \xrightarrow{\sim} V\( an isomorphism. Setting \)\psi_0 \colon V \to A$
515	  to be the composition $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi|_U \circ (f^{(g)}|_U)^{-1}
516	  \colon V \to U \to A\( produces a rational map \)J \dashrightarrow A$.
517	
518	  The target \(A\) is an abelian variety and the source \(J\) is smooth over
519	  \(\bar k\) (it is itself an abelian variety, hence nonsingular). Milne's
520	  Theorem~3.2 (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) therefore promotes
521	  \(\psi_0\) to a unique regular morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) extending
522	  \(\psi_0\) on \(V\). Uniqueness of \(\psi\) follows from the
523	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle: two regular morphisms
524	  \(J \to A\) agreeing on a dense open are equal.
525	\end{proof}
526	
527	\section{Proof of the universal property}
528	\label{sec:albaneseup_proof}
529	
530	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
531	% "PROOF.  Consider the map
532	%    \(\)(P_1,\ldots,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\(\)
533	%  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\).  It
534	%  therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
535	%  shows to be a regular map.  It is clear from the construction that
536	%  \(\psi\circ f^P=\varphi\) (note that \(f^P\) is the composite of $Q\mapsto
537	%  Q+(g-1)P\colon C\to C^{(g)}\( with \)f^{(g)}\colon C^{(g)}\to J$).  In
538	%  particular, \(\psi\) maps 0 to 0, and (I 1.2) shows that it is therefore
539	%  a homomorphism.  If \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism such that
540	%  \(\psi'\circ f^P=\varphi\), then \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on
541	%  \(f^P(C)+\cdots+f^P(C)\) (\(g\) copies), which is the whole of \(J\)."
542	\begin{proof}[Proof of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}]
543	  
544	  The proof follows Milne's Proposition~6.1 verbatim, using the four
545	  sub-lemmas of this chapter:
546	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} (Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\)),
547	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} (symmetrisation of \(\varphi\) to
548	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)),
549	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian} (birational morphism
550	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\)), and
551	  \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} (descent of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
552	  \varphi\( to a regular morphism \)\psi \colon J \to A$ via
553	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}).
554	  \begin{enumerate}
555	    \item \emph{Symmetrisation.} Consider the map \(C^g \to A\) sending
556	      \((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\)
557	      (Milne's typeset proof writes \(\sum_i \psi(P_i)\) in this line, but
558	      the construction is the symmetrisation of the input \(\varphi\); we
559	      use the unambiguous symbol \(\varphi\) throughout). Since \(A\) is
560	      commutative this map is \(S_g\)-symmetric. By
561	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} it factors through the
562	      symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\), defining a regular morphism
563	      \[
564	        \widetilde \psi \;:=\; \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi
565	        \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A.
566	      \]
567	    \item \emph{Descent to a regular morphism on \(J\).} By
568	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, the morphism
569	      \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) is birational. The
570	      composite \(\widetilde \psi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1}\) is, a priori,
571	      only a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\). By
572	      \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} -- whose proof is an
573	      application of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne
574	      Theorem~I~3.2, rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
575	      abelian variety is regular) -- this rational map extends uniquely
576	      to a regular morphism
577	      \[
578	        \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A.
579	      \]
580	    \item \emph{Factorisation \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\).}
581	      Milne's identification: \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the composite
582	      \[
583	        C \;\xrightarrow{\;Q \mapsto Q + (g-1) P_0\;}\;
584	        \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\xrightarrow{\;f^{(g)}\;}\; J,
585	      \]
586	      where the first arrow sends a point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) to the
587	      effective divisor \(Q + (g-1) P_0 \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\)
588	      (its image under \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is
589	      $[\mathcal O_C(Q + (g-1) P_0 - g P_0)] = [\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)]
590	      = \iota_{P_0}(Q)$, matching \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}). On
591	      \(\bar k\)-points,
592	      \[
593	        \psi(\iota_{P_0}(Q)) = \widetilde\psi(Q + (g-1) P_0)
594	          = \varphi(Q) + (g-1)\varphi(P_0) = \varphi(Q),
595	      \]
596	      using \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\) and that \(\eta_A\) is the identity of
597	      \(A\). Hence \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) on \(\bar k\)-points,
598	      and therefore \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) as morphisms of
599	      \(\bar k\)-schemes (both sides are regular morphisms \(C \to A\) from
600	      a reduced separated scheme to a separated scheme, agreeing on
601	      dense \(\bar k\)-points, hence agreeing everywhere by the
602	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle).
603	    \item \emph{Homomorphism property.} The morphism \(\psi\) sends
604	      \(\eta_J\) to \(\eta_A\): indeed,
605	      \(\psi(\eta_J) = \psi(\iota_{P_0}(P_0)) = \varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\).
606	      A regular morphism of abelian varieties sending the identity to
607	      the identity is a homomorphism of group schemes, by Milne
608	      Corollary~I~1.2 (a consequence of the Rigidity Lemma
609	      \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, see
610	      \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, Corollary~1.2). Therefore
611	      \(\psi\) is a homomorphism of group schemes \(J \to A\).
612	    \item \emph{Uniqueness.} Suppose \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism
613	      with \(\psi' \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\). Then \(\psi\) and
614	      \(\psi'\) agree on the set-theoretic image $\iota_{P_0}(C) \subseteq
615	      J$. Both are homomorphisms, so they agree on the subgroup
616	      generated by \(\iota_{P_0}(C)\) -- which contains the \(g\)-fold sum
617	      \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) copies). The
618	      \(g\)-fold sum is the image of
619	      \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J\),
620	      which descends to \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), which
621	      is surjective by \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}.
622	      Therefore \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on a dense open subset of \(J\)
623	      (in fact on all of \(J\) as a set), hence everywhere by the
624	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle.
625	  \end{enumerate}
626	  This completes the proof.
627	\end{proof}
628	
629	\section{Wiring for the positive-genus Albanese witness}
630	\label{sec:albaneseup_witness_wiring}
631	
632	\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} supplies the universal-factorisation
633	field \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\) of an Albanese witness for \((C, P_0)\) in the
634	sense of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}); the remaining
635	fields are supplied by A.3:
636	\begin{itemize}
637	  \item \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) (the underlying scheme).
638	  \item \(\texttt{grpObj}\): the group-object structure on \(J\) obtained from the
639	    abelian-group structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(T)\) for varying \(T\), refined to a
640	    group object in the category of \(\bar k\)-schemes (A.3, A.2.c group-scheme
641	    refinement).
642	  \item \(\texttt{proper}\): properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec \bar k\) from
643	    the closed-immersion property of the identity component in the proper
644	    representing Picard scheme (A.3; cf.\
645	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_proper}'s discussion).
646	  \item \(\texttt{smoothGenus}\): smoothness of relative dimension \(g(C)\) from
647	    the deformation-theoretic tangent-space identification with $H^1(C,
648	    \mathcal O_C)\(, which has dimension \)g(C)$ by Riemann--Roch.
649	  \item \(\texttt{geomIrred}\): geometric irreducibility of the identity
650	    component of a \(\bar k\)-group scheme locally of finite type (Kleiman
651	    Lem.~lem:agps and Prp.~prp:pic0; cf.\
652	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}'s discussion).
653	  \item \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\): the present
654	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}, applied at each
655	    \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\).
656	\end{itemize}
657	
658	The witness so produced is over \(\bar k\). The Galois-descent step from $\bar
659	k\( back to the original base field \)k$ -- packaged at
660	\cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f -- closes the loop and supplies the
661	\(k\)-side Albanese witness consumed by \cref{def:Jacobian}.
662

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  
150	\leanok
151	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
152	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
153	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
154	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
155	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
156	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
157	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
158	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
159	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
160	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
161	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
162	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
163	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
164	\end{proof}

166	\section{Projective dimension}
167	\label{sec:ab_pd}
168	
169	\begin{definition}
170	
171	\leanok
172	  [Projective dimension]
173	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
174	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
175	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
176	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
177	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
178	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
179	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
180	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
181	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
182	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
183	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
184	  \[
185	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
186	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
187	  \]
188	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
189	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
190	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
191	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
192	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
193	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
194	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
195	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
196	\end{definition}
197	
198	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
199	\begin{itemize}
200	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
201	    iff \(M\) is free);
202	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
203	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
204	\end{itemize}
205	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
206	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
207	
208	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
209	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
210	
211	\begin{lemma}
212	
213	\leanok
214	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
215	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
216	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
217	  
218	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
219	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
220	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
221	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
222	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
223	  %  \begin{enumerate}
224	  %  \item
225	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
226	  %  \item
227	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
228	  %  \item
229	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
230	  %  \end{enumerate}"
231	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
232	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
233	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
234	  \(R\)-modules. Then
235	  \begin{enumerate}
236	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
237	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
238	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
239	  \end{enumerate}
240	\end{lemma}
241	
242	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
243	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
244	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
245	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
246	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
247	%  from Lemma REF."
248	\begin{proof}
249	  
250	  
251	  
252	  
253	  
254	\leanok
255	  By \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
256	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
257	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
258	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
259	  \[
260	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
261	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
262	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
263	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
264	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
265	  \]
266	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
267	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
268	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
269	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
270	\end{proof}

273	\label{sec:ab_main}
274	
275	\begin{theorem}
276	
277	\leanok
278	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
279	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
280	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
281	  
282	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
283	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
284	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
285	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
286	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
287	  %  \(\)
288	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
289	  %  \(\)"
290	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
291	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
292	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
293	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
294	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
295	  \[
296	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
297	  \]
298	\end{theorem}
299

301	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
302	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
303	%  \(\)
304	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
305	%  \(\)
306	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
307	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
308	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
309	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
310	%  Proposition REF we see that
311	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
312	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
313	%  \begin{align*}
314	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
315	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
316	%  \ldots,\\
317	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
318	%  \end{align*}
319	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
320	%  \begin{align*}
321	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
322	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
323	%  \ldots,\\
324	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
325	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
326	%  \end{align*}
327	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
328	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
329	%
330	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
331	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
332	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
333	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
334	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
335	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
336	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
337	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
338	%  \(\)
339	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
340	%  \(\)
341	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
342	%  shows that
343	%  \(\)
344	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
345	%  M/xM \to 0
346	%  \(\)
347	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
348	%  By Lemma REF we have
349	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
350	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
351	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
352	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
353	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
354	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
355	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
356	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
357	\begin{proof}
358	  
359	  
360	  
361	\leanok
362	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
363	
364	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
365	  finite free resolution
366	  \[
367	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
368	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
369	  \]
370	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
371	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
372	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
373	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
374	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
375	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
376	  sequences
377	  \[
378	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
379	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
380	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
381	  \]
382	  and applying \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
383	  yields the chain of inequalities
384	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
385	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
386	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
387	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
388	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
389	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
390	
391	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
392	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
393	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
394	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
395	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
396	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
397	  \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
398	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
399	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
400	  \[
401	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
402	  \]
403	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
404	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
405	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
406	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
407	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
408	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
409	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
410	  \[
411	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
412	  \]
413	  Substituting yields
414	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
415	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
416	\end{proof}

445	  depth-drop on both \(R\) and \(M\) after quotienting by an

447	  the minimal resolution to extract the \(R/(x)\)-minimal resolution

449	  \begin{enumerate}
450	    \item Minimal-resolution carving (Stacks

454	    \item ``What is exact'' criterion (Stacks 00MF /

473	\begin{lemma}
474	
475	\leanok
476	  [Depth drops by one under a regular element]
477	  \label{lem:depth_drops_by_one}

483	  \(R\)-module \(M\), and an \(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\),
484	  \[

486	  \]

522	substrate builds; it is no longer an open work-list.

524	\medskip
525	The inductive step
526	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
527	of the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula was originally gated, against an
528	earlier pinned Mathlib snapshot, by three absent substrate ingredients
529	(gaps (1)--(3)) plus a fourth in hand
530	(gap (4), \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}). This subsection records the
531	decomposition that the proof block of
532	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} folded into four bullets:
533	its purpose was to make the dependency graph and the per-gap effort

536	
537	\paragraph{Sequencing constraint.} The three open gaps are
538	\emph{not} mutually independent at the substrate level. Gap (3)
539	(snake-lemma carving of the minimal resolution by an
540	\(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\)) needs gap (1)
541	(minimal-resolution normalisation: every finite free resolution of
542	a finite module over a Noetherian local ring trims to a minimal one
543	of the same projective dimension) as input, because the snake-lemma
544	output is only known to be a \emph{minimal} resolution of \(M/xM\)
545	over \(R/(x)\) of the same length when the input resolution is itself
546	minimal. Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF, the ``what is exact'' criterion in
547	terms of depths of \(r\)-minors of the transition matrices) is
548	independent of gaps (1) and (3) at the substrate level: it is a
549	self-contained criterion on a complex of finite free modules and
550	feeds the closure assembly without going through gap (1) or gap (3).
551

604	The gap (1) estimate is anchored to Bruns--Herzog,
605	\emph{Cohen--Macaulay Rings}, Construction~1.5.18 together with the
606	minimal-resolution induction (\S1.5): the trimming algorithm is a
607	finite-rank linear-algebra computation followed by induction on the
608	length of the resolution. The gap (2) estimate reflects the chain
609	Bruns--Herzog Theorem~1.4.13 \(\to\) Eagon--Northcott
610	\(\to\) Buchsbaum--Eisenbud's exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
611	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}), which characterises exactness of a
612	complex of finite free modules in terms of depths of the ideals of
613	\(r\)-minors of the transition matrices. Gap (2) is the largest of
614	the three open substrates and is a natural candidate for an upstream
615	Mathlib contribution rather than a project-internal proof.
616	
617	\paragraph{Gap (3) OBVIATED iter-200.} The iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB
618	pivot (\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the literal
619	\(\texttt{ChainComplex}\,\mathbb N\) carrier with the abstract
620	\(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES-descent pattern; under this
621	pattern, minimality of the resolution is \emph{not} used as input
622	anywhere in the inductive step. Consequently the snake-lemma carving
623	of the minimal resolution (gap (3)) is no longer a substrate
624	requirement: only gap (2) (\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow
625	\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)) remains the binding gap for
626	the closure of \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}, and the
627	per-syzygy step of gap (1) is consumed only through the SES at
628	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) (no minimality input required).
629	See \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot} for the SES-descent details.
630	
631	\paragraph{Gap (2) Stacks 00MF proof recipe.} The
632	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
633	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}) reads: a complex
634	\(\dots \to R^{r_2} \xrightarrow{\varphi_2} R^{r_1}
635	 \xrightarrow{\varphi_1} R^{r_0}\) of finite free modules over a
636	Noetherian local ring \(R\) is exact at \(R^{r_i}\) (for \(i \geq 1\))
637	if and only if \(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi_{i+1} + \texttt{rank}\,\varphi_i
638	= r_i\) and \(\texttt{depth}\,I(\varphi_i) \geq i\), where
639	\(I(\varphi)\) denotes the ideal of \(r\)-minors of \(\varphi\) for
640	\(r = \texttt{rank}\,\varphi\). The recipe for an iter-201+ prover:
641	(a) define \(I(\varphi) \subseteq R\) as the ideal of all
642	\(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi\)-minors of \(\varphi\) (Mathlib supports
643	this via \(\texttt{Matrix.minors}\) and \(\texttt{Ideal.span}\) over the
644	finite index set of \(\binom{n}{r}\) tuples); (b) prove the depth
645	lower bound via induction on \(i\) using a Koszul-complex argument
646	on the regular sequence implicit in the exactness data; (c) derive
647	\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow \texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)
648	as a corollary by taking the start \(\dots \to R^{r_1} \to R^{r_0} \to
649	M\) of a minimal resolution and applying the criterion at \(i = 1\).
650	Estimated \(\sim 150-200\) LOC project-side; Mathlib upstream PR
651	candidate.
652	
653	\paragraph{Closure assembly (iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot).} The
654	iter-200 substrate landing in \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}
655	replaces the original (1)+(3)-dependent assembly with an SES-descent
656	recipe: given an \(M\) with \(\texttt{pd}_R(M) = k+1\), the
657	per-syzygy step of gap (1)
658	(\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}) supplies a
659	surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\); the SES
660	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) plus the iter-200 helpers
661	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker\_of\_surjection}\) +
662	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_succ\_of\_hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker}\)
663	give \(\texttt{pd}_R(\ker f) = k\) exactly; gap (2) (the
664	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud criterion at the start of a minimal resolution)
665	delivers \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\); the iter-200
666	helper
667	\(\texttt{depth\_ker\_ge\_min\_of\_surjection\_finite\_localRing}\)
668	gives \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f \geq \min(\texttt{depth}\,R,
669	\texttt{depth}\,M + 1) = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\); a complementary LES
670	upper bound (or the IH applied to \(\ker f\) under the strict
671	descent of \(\texttt{pd}\)) gives the matching upper bound. Combine
672	to get \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\), then apply
673	the IH (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\)) for
674	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), which rearranges
675	to \((k+1) + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) as required.
676	Estimated closure-assembly cost (gaps in hand): \(\sim 50-80\) LOC
677	inductive code.
678	
679	\paragraph{Iter budget refinement.} Aggregating the per-gap
680	estimates with the closure assembly: gap (1) at 1--2 iters, gap (2)
681	at 2--3 iters, gap (3) at 1--2 iters, and the closure assembly
682	at 1 iter gives a total remaining iter budget of \textbf{5--8 iters}
683	for \cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}. The schedule may
684	parallelise across the gap (2) branch and the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3)
685	chain --- so wall-clock could be as low as
686	\(\max(\text{gap (2)},\, \text{gap (1)} + \text{gap (3)}) +
687	\text{closure}\), i.e.\ \(\max(2\text{--}3,\, 2\text{--}4) + 1\),

689	sequencing constraint (gap (3) requires gap (1)) is hard; the gap
690	(2) branch is genuinely independent of that chain and could be

692	This estimate refines the earlier ``~6--12 iters'' coarse budget

698	\subsection{Gap (1) first-step substrate (iter-199): per-syzygy
699	  minimal surjection from a finite free module}
700	\label{subsec:ab_gap1_first_step}
701	
702	\begin{lemma}
703	
704	\leanok
705	  [Per-syzygy minimal surjection from \(R^n\) for finite modules
706	    over a local ring]
707	  \label{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
708	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
709	  \uses{def:depth}
710	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
711	  % (lemma-add-trivial-complex; minimal-resolution carving substrate)
712	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L24590-L24623).
713	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
714	  % "Suppose $R$ is a ring. Let
715	  %     \ldots
716	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 1}}
717	  %     R^{n_i}
718	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_i}
719	  %     R^{n_{i-1}}
720	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i-1}}
721	  %     \ldots
722	  %  be a complex of finite free $R$-modules. Suppose that for some $i$
723	  %  some matrix coefficient of the map $\varphi_i$ is invertible.
724	  %  Then the displayed complex is isomorphic to the direct sum of a
725	  %  complex
726	  %     \ldots \to
727	  %     R^{n_{i + 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 2}}
728	  %     R^{n_{i + 1}} \to
729	  %     R^{n_i - 1} \to
730	  %     R^{n_{i - 1} - 1} \to
731	  %     R^{n_{i - 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i - 2}}
732	  %     R^{n_{i - 3}} \to
733	  %     \ldots
734	  %  and the complex $\ldots \to 0 \to R \to R \to 0 \to \ldots$ where
735	  %  the map $R \to R$ is the identity map."
736	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
737	    (\texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}).}
738	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
739	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
740	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
741	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
742	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
743	  \begin{enumerate}
744	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
745	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
746	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
747	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
748	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
749	      summands).
750	  \end{enumerate}
751	\end{lemma}
752	\begin{proof}
753	
754	\leanok
755	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
756	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
757	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
758	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
759	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
760	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
761	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
762	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
763	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
764	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
765	
766	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
767	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
768	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
769	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
770	
771	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
772	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
773	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
774	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
775	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
776	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
777	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
778	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
779	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
780	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
781	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
782	\end{proof}
783	
784	This lemma is the \emph{per-syzygy} step of gap (1)'s
785	minimal-resolution carving: given a finite \(R\)-module \(M\)
786	over a local ring \(R\), it produces a minimal surjection
787	\(R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\). Iter-200's ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot
788	(\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the original
789	\(\mathbb N\)-recursive \(\texttt{ChainComplex}\) assembly target
790	with abstract \(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES descent: only
791	the per-syzygy SES is consumed; the iterated chain-complex carrier
792	is not needed.
793

871	[Depth lower bound on the kernel of a finite-free surjection]
872	  \label{lem:depth_ker_ge_min_of_surjection_finite_localRing}

889	plus gap (2) (Stacks 00MF, the \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)

895	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_succ_of_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker}

897	\ref{lem:depth_ker_ge_min_of_surjection_finite_localRing} plus gap (2)

899	\(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\); the inductive
