902	binding remaining substrate is gap (2) (\(\sim 150\)--\(200\) LOC,
903	Mathlib upstream PR candidate per the recipe in
904	\ref{subsec:succ_pd_gap_sequence}).
905	
906	\paragraph{Iter-201 Path B closure recipe (LES-injectivity +
907	matrix-collapse).} The iter-201 \texttt{ab-stacks00mf} mathlib-
908	analogist verdict identifies an alternative closure that sidesteps
909	gap (2): instead of building Stacks 00MF as a standalone substrate,
910	split \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} into an
911	\emph{inductive step} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)) and a
912	\emph{base case} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\)). Each piece uses only
913	already-existing LES infrastructure plus one new
914	\(\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC matrix-collapse helper.
915	
916	\textit{Inductive step \(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)
917	\((\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC).} From
918	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker_of_surjection} +
919	the contrapositive of
920	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_succ_of_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker},
921	\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\) exactly. Strong induction gives
922	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), hence
923	\(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f < \texttt{depth}\,R\) since \(k \geq 1\).
924	Lower bound \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\):
925	\(\texttt{depth\_of\_short\_exact}\) part (2). Upper bound
926	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\): by
927	contradiction --- assume \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\),
928	look at the LES of \(\texttt{Ext}^*(\kappa, -)\) at
929	\(i = \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\), infer
930	\(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,\ker f}(\kappa, R^n) \neq 0\)
931	(forcing \(\texttt{depth}\,R \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\)),
932	contradicting the IH.

1032	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
1033	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
1034	
1035	\begin{definition}
1036	
1037	\leanok
1038	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
1039	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
1040	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
1041	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
1042	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
1043	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1044	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
1045	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
1046	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
1047	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
1048	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
1049	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
1050	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
1051	\end{definition}
1052

1054	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper

1056	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
1057	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the

1071	\begin{corollary}
1072	
1073	\leanok
1074	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
1075	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
1076	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
1077	  
1078	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
1079	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
1080	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1081	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
1082	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
1083	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
1084	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
1085	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
1086	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
1087	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
1088	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
1089	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
1090	\end{corollary}
1091	
1092	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1093	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
1094	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
1095	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
1096	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
1097	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
1098	\begin{proof}
1099	  
1100	  
1101	  
1102	  
1103	  
1104	\leanok
1105	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
1106	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
1107	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
1108	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
1109	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
1110	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
1111	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
1112	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
1113	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
1114	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
1115	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
1116	
1117	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
1118	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
1119	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
1120	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
1121	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
1122	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
1123	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
1124	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
1125	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
1126	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
1127	\end{proof}

1129	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
1130	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
1131	
1132	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
1133	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
1134	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
1135	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
1136	
1137	\begin{enumerate}
1138	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
1139	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
1140	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
1141	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
1142	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
1143	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
1144	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
1145	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
1146	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
1147	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
1148	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
1149	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
1150	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
1151	\end{enumerate}
1152	
1153	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
1154	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
1155	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
1156	Corollary~REF are formalized in
1157	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
1158	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.

520	\begin{proof}
521	
522	
523	\leanok
524	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
525	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
526	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
527	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
528	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
529	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
530	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
531	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
532	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
533	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
534	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
535	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
536	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
537	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
538	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
539	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
540	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
541	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
542	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
543	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
544	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
545	
546	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
547	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
548	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
549	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
550	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
551	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
552	\end{proof}

555

558	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
559	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
560	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
561	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
562	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
563	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
564	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
565	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
566	  morphism
567	  \[
568	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
569	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
570	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
571	  \]
572	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
573	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
574	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
575	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
576	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
577	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
578	\end{lemma}
579	
580	\begin{proof}
581	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
582	  \leanok
583	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
584	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
585	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
586	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
587	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
588	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
589	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
590	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
591	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
592	  isomorphism}
593	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
594	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
595	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
596	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
597	  therefore extension of scalars along
598	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
599	
600	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
601	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
602	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
603	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
604	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
605	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
606	  the abstract pullback functor.
607	
608	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
609	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
610	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
611	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
612	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
613	   \;\cong\;
614	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
615	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
616	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
617	  comparison
618	  \[
619	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
620	      \;\longrightarrow\;
621	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
622	      \;\otimes\;
623	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
624	  \]
625	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
626	
627	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
628	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
629	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
630	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
631	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
632	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
633	  % "\begin{lemma}
634	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
635	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
636	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
637	  % \begin{enumerate}
638	  % \item The functor
639	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
640	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
641	  % \item The functor
642	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
643	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
644	  %  [\dots]
645	  % \end{enumerate}
646	  % \end{lemma}
647	  %  [\dots]
648	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
649	  % of functors, see
650	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
651	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
652	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
653	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
654	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
655	
656	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
657	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
658	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
659	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
660	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
661	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
662	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
663	
664	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
665	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
666	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
667	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
668	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
669	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
670	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
671	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
672	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
673	  \otimes_S N)\).
674	
675	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
676	  \[
677	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
678	  \]
679	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
680	  presheaf-level adjunction
681	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
682	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
683	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
684	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
685	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
686	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
687	  composing the resulting comparison with
688	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
689	
690	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
691	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
692	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
693	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
694	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
695	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
696	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
697	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
698	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
699	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
700	
701	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
702	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
703	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
704	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
705	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
706	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
707	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
708	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
709	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
710	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
711	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
712	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
713	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
714	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
715	\end{proof}

717	\begin{lemma}
718	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
719	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
720	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
721	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
722	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
723	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
724	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
725	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
726	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
727	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
728	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
729	
730	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
731	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
732	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
733	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
734	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
735	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
736	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
737	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
738	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
739	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
740	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
741	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
742	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
743	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
744	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
745	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
746	
747	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
748	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
749	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,
750	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}\) equips the presheaf-level
751	  restriction-of-scalars functor
752	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal
753	  structure whenever the base-change comparison \(\alpha\) is \emph{sectionwise
754	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
755	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via
756	  \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\). It is to be distinguished from the
757	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
758	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in
759	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}: the present declaration is the
760	  \emph{presheaf}-level, \emph{bijective}-comparison form, the one the substrate lift
761	  actually consumes (where the comparison map
762	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
763	  morphism but is not syntactically a \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\)).
764	
765	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they
766	  are self-contained presheaf-level results and are natural upstream-PR candidates.
767	\end{lemma}

769	\begin{lemma}
770	
771	\leanok
772	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
773	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
774	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
775	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
776	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
777	  product: the canonical map
778	  \[
779	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
780	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
781	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
782	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
783	  \]
784	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
785	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
786	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
787	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
788	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
789	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
790	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
791	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
792	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
793	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
794	\end{lemma}
795	
796	\begin{lemma}
797	
798	[Strong-monoidal upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism (ModuleCat-level H2 core)]
799	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}
800	  \lean{restrictScalars_isIso_μ, restrictScalars_isIso_ε,
801	        restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv,
802	        restrictScalars_isIso_μ_of_bijective,
803	        restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective}
804	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
805	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings and let
806	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom} :
807	  \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,S \to \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\) be the associated
808	  restriction-of-scalars functor, equipped with its canonical \emph{lax}-monoidal
809	  structure (the lax tensorator \(\mu\) and lax unit \(\varepsilon\) supplied by
810	  Mathlib). Then:
811	  \begin{enumerate}
812	    \item the lax tensorator
813	      \(\mu_{M, N} : \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e N
814	      \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M \otimes_S N)\) is an isomorphism
815	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu}\));
816	    \item the lax unit
817	      \(\varepsilon : \mathbf{1}_R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(\mathbf{1}_S)\)
818	      is an isomorphism (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon}\));
819	    \item consequently \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) is
820	      \emph{strong} monoidal: it carries the packaged structure
821	      \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
822	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\)).
823	  \end{enumerate}
824	  The same two invertibility statements hold, more flexibly, under the hypothesis that
825	  the underlying ring homomorphism is merely \emph{bijective} rather than literally of
826	  the form \(e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) for a packaged ring equivalence \(e\)
827	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu\_of\_bijective}\),
828	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon\_of\_bijective}\)); these bijective
829	  forms are the ones consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the relevant
830	  comparison map \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective
831	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) morphism but is not syntactically a
832	  \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\).
833	\end{lemma}
834	
835	\begin{proof}
836	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
837	  Statements (1) and (2) are the categorical repackaging of
838	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: when the ground-ring map is a ring
839	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
840	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
841	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of
842	  \(\mu_{M, N}\) is the additive map \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\) of
843	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}, verified to be two-sided inverse to
844	  the canonical \(R\)-linear \(\mu_{M, N}\); for (2) the unit comparison
845	  \(\mathbf{1}_R = R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e S\) is the additive identification
846	  induced by \(e\). Strong monoidality (3) is then the Mathlib upgrade of a
847	  lax-monoidal functor all of whose structure maps are isomorphisms
848	  (\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\)), packaging \(\mu^{-1}, \varepsilon^{-1}\)
849	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).
850	  The bijective-hypothesis forms are proved identically, replacing ``\(e\) is a ring
851	  equivalence'' by ``the underlying ring homomorphism is bijective'' and reconstructing
852	  the inverse ground-ring map from bijectivity; this is what lets the presheaf-level
853	  lift feed in a section-wise-bijective comparison map directly.
854	
855	  These declarations constitute the \emph{ModuleCat-level H2}: strong
856	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism commutes with \(\otimes\). The
857	  one \emph{remaining} H2 step is the bounded, no-Mathlib-gap \emph{presheaf} lift:
858	  assembling \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
859	  section-by-section via \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\), using a
860	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-iso\,\(\Rightarrow\)\,bijective bridge to invoke the
861	  bijective forms above at each section, together with the
862	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) iso-iff-app-iso reflection to lift section-wise
863	  invertibility to the presheaf level; once that lift is in place,
864	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) is monoidal. This presheaf H2 lift is bounded and
865	  has no Mathlib-absent ingredient; the sole genuinely Mathlib-absent piece of
866	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is the presheaf-level pushforward adjunction
867	  (``H1'').
868	\end{proof}

870	\begin{lemma}
871	
872	
873	\leanok
874	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
875	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
876	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
877	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
878	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
879	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
880	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
881	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
882	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
883	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
884	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
885	\end{lemma}
886	
887	\begin{proof}
888	
889	
890	
891	\leanok
892	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
893	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
894	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
895	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
896	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
897	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
898	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
899	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
900	\end{proof}

902	\emph{Closed standalone result --- off the critical path.} The following lemma
903	\emph{is} formalized and sorry-free; it is not, however, consumed by the relative
904	Picard group law. The associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized
905	\emph{unconditionally} through the varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation
906	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
907	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which closes the single left-whiskering
908	obligation for \emph{all} modules with no flatness and no local-triviality
909	hypothesis. The sectionwise-flatness whiskering statement below is therefore
910	neither the live whiskering-stability route nor on the critical path: it is a valid,
911	self-contained result about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms,
912	retained for potential reuse. (Unlike the genuinely abandoned
913	\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, it is a closed lemma, not a stub to be skipped.)
914	
915	\begin{lemma}
916	% Closed, sorry-free standalone result (flat whiskering). Off the critical path:
917	% the associator is realized via the d.2 stalk-tensor route
918	% (lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk), not via this flat lemma.
919	
920	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
921	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
922	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
923	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
924	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
925	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
926	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
927	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
928	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
929	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
930	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
931	  left-whiskered morphism
932	  \[
933	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
934	  \]
935	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
936	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
937	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
938	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
939	
940	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
941	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
942	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
943	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability obligation
944	  for the unconditional associator is closed for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering
945	  object \(F\) by the stalk--tensor commutation
946	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
947	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which never invokes the
948	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness
949	  condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the
950	  site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility
951	  or local triviality, which are affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma
952	  is therefore retained as an independent, closed result, superseded on the critical
953	  path by the d.2 route of \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}.
954	\end{lemma}
955	
956	\begin{proof}
957	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
958	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
959	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
960	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
961	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
962	
963	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
964	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
965	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
966	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
967	  \[
968	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
969	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
970	  \]
971	
972	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
973	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
974	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
975	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
976	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
977	
978	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
979	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
980	  linear maps (Mathlib
981	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
982	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
983	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
984	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
985	
986	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
987	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
988	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
989	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
990	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
991	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
992	\end{proof}
993	
994	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma
995	belongs to the abandoned coherent-monoidal-category / sheafification-whiskering
996	approach to the associator. The group law on iso-classes consumes only the
997	\emph{existence} of the coherence isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}),
998	and the associator is realized as the sheafification transport of the presheaf
999	associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), whose single open left-whisker obligation
1000	is closed by the d.2 route of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}. The
1001	\emph{full} \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1002	monoidal-category packaging is not required. This block is retained for the historical
1003	record only and must \emph{not} be formalized.
1004	
1005	\begin{lemma}
1006	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1007	
1008	\leanok
1009	[Sheafification inverts a localizer morphism of presheaves of modules]
1010	  \label{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}
1011	  Let \(R_0\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a
1012	  Grothendieck topology \(J\), let \(R_{sh}\) be its sheafification, and let
1013	  \(\alpha : R_0 \to R_{sh}.\mathrm{obj}\) be the (locally bijective) comparison
1014	  map of presheaves of rings. Let \(f : A \to B\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1015	  \(R_0\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1016	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\) lies in the sheafification
1017	  localizer class \(J.W\) --- equivalently, is locally bijective for \(J\). Then
1018	  the presheaf-of-modules sheafification functor
1019	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\,\alpha\) sends \(f\) to an
1020	  isomorphism:
1021	  \[
1022	    (\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f
1023	    \quad\text{is an isomorphism.}
1024	  \]
1025	  This is a closed go/no-go bridge --- it converts ``\,a morphism lies in
1026	  \(J.W\)\,'' into ``\,its sheafification is invertible\,''. Under route~(e) the
1027	  associator is the API-derived one (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), so this bridge is
1028	  retained as a supplement rather than as the associator's load-bearing step.
1029	\end{lemma}
1030	
1031	\begin{proof}
1032	
1033	\leanok
1034	  This is a one-morphism reading of the structural identity that the
1035	  sheafification of presheaves of \(R_0\)-modules \emph{is} the localization of
1036	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R_0\) at the class
1037	  \(J.W.\mathtt{inverseImage}\,(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0)\) of morphisms whose
1038	  underlying map of presheaves of abelian groups is locally bijective. Concretely,
1039	  the underlying \(\mathtt{AddCommGrp}\)-valued sheafification inverts exactly the
1040	  locally bijective morphisms, and Mathlib's
1041	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.inverseImage\_W\_toPresheaf\_eq\_inverseImage\_%
1042	  isomorphisms}\) identifies the preimage of \(J.W\) under
1043	  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0\) with the preimage of the class of isomorphisms under
1044	  the module-level sheafification functor. Reading that equality of morphism
1045	  classes at the single morphism \(f\): the hypothesis
1046	  \(J.W\,\bigl((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\bigr)\) places \(f\) in
1047	  that preimage, hence \((\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f\) is an
1048	  isomorphism. No new construction is required beyond instantiating the existing
1049	  Mathlib identity at one morphism; this lemma is a closed thin wrapper, retained
1050	  as a supplement under route~(e).
1051	\end{proof}
1052	
1053	\section{Route~(e): the \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal structure}
1054	\label{sec:tensorobj_route_e}
1055	
1056	\emph{Full-monoidal-category construction --- off path, not to be formalized.} The
1057	construction below realizes a \emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on all of
1058	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) by instantiating Mathlib's
1059	localization-of-monoidal-categories API. It was the original plan for supplying the
1060	associator. The relative Picard group law, however, is a structure on
1061	\emph{isomorphism classes} of invertible sheaves and consumes only the
1062	\emph{existence} of the associator, unitor and braiding isomorphisms
1063	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, built by hand as the scheme-theoretic
1064	analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), not off a coherent monoidal
1065	category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)). The associator is obtained as the sheafification
1066	transport of the presheaf associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), whose
1067	\emph{single} open left-whisker obligation is closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
1068	commutation (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk,%
1069	lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); it uses \emph{no} \emph{full}
1070	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1071	monoidal category. Accordingly the \emph{full monoidal-category} target of this
1072	section --- the \emph{pair} of whiskering-stability fields (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W})
1073	and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) --- is off
1074	path and must not be re-attempted; it is kept for the historical record. The d.1/d.2
1075	stalk apparatus it shares with the live single whisker obligation is \emph{not}
1076	vestigial, however: it is on the critical path through
1077	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1078	
1079	The monoidal structure on the substrate is realized by \emph{instantiating}
1080	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
1081	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}): apply
1082	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) to the already-monoidal
1083	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the
1084	sheafification localizer \(J.W\). The single new input the API requires is the
1085	instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), whose two fields are the
1086	whiskering-stability lemmas below.
1087	
1088	\paragraph{What the group law consumes.} The group law is assembled directly on
1089	isomorphism classes of \emph{locally trivial} (invertible) sheaves
1090	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard
1091	group \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1092	(\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). It needs only the \emph{existence} of
1093	the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1094	and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, together with \(\otimes\)-invertibility
1095	supplying inverses; it uses \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance, \emph{no}
1096	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\).
1097	It does, however, consume the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, which is
1098	realized as the sheafification transport of the presheaf associator and whose
1099	\emph{single} open left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
107	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
109	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
118	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
119	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
120	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
121	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
122	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
128	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
131	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
133	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
137	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
143	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
147	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
148	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
149	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
186	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
193	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of
202	  finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of \(C/k\).
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  
224	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
225	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
226	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
227	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
228	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
229	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
232	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
233	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
234	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
235	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
236	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
237	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
238	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
239	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
240	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
241	  \(k\)-schemes.
242	
243	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
244	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
245	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
246	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
247	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
248	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
249	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
250	  \(k\)-scheme.
251	
252	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
253	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
254	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
255	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
256	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
257	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
258	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
259	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
260	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
261	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
262	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
263	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
264	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
265	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
266	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
267	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
268	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
270	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
271	  smooth and proper.
272	
273	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
274	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
275	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
276	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
277	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
278	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
279	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
280	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
281	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
282	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
283	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
284	  \(X = C\) complete case.
285	\end{proof}
286	
287	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
288	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
289	
290	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
291	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
292	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
293	representing Picard piece.
294	
295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
296	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
297	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
300	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
301	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
303	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
304	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
305	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
306	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
307	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
308	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
309	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
310	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
311	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
312	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
313	  \begin{enumerate}
314	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
315	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
316	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
317	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
318	      and proper map.
319	  \end{enumerate}
320	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
321	  is representable by a smooth map.
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
325	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
326	% structural heart of step 4.)
327	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
328	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
329	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
330	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
331	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
332	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
333	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
334	%
335	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
336	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
337	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
338	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
339	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
340	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
341	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
342	% [\dots]
343	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
344	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
345	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
346	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
347	%
348	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
349	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
350	% [\dots]
351	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
352	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
353	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
354	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
355	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
356	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
357	% represents \(P\)."
358	\begin{proof}
359	
360	\leanok
361	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
362	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
363	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
364	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
365	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
366	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
367	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
368	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
369	  closed embedding.
370	
371	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
372	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
373	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
374	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
375	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
376	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
377	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
378	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
379	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
380	
381	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
382	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
383	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
384	
385	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
386	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
387	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
388	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
389	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
390	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
391	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
392	\end{proof}

394	\section{Group-scheme structure}
395	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
396	
397	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
398	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
399	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
400	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
401	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
402	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
403	
404	\begin{theorem}\leanok
405	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
406	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
408	  
409	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
410	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
411	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
412	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
413	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
414	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
415	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
416	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
417	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
418	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
419	  %  topologies  by
420	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
421	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
422	  %   \end{dfn}"
423	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
424	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
425	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
426	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
427	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
428	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
429	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
430	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
431	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
432	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
433	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
434	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
435	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
436	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
437	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
438	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
439	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
440	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
441	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
442	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
443	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
444	  scheme locally of finite type.
445	\end{theorem}
446	
447	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
448	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
449	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
450	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
451	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
452	% records the standard transfer.
453	\begin{proof}
454	  
455	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
456	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
457	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
458	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
459	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
460	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
461	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
462	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
463	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
464	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
465	  in the value category).
466	
467	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
468	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
469	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
470	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
471	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
472	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
473	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
474	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
475	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
476	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
477	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
478	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
479	\end{proof}
480	
481	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
482	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
483	
484	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
485	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
486	
487	\begin{definition}\leanok
488	[The Picard scheme]
489	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
490	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
491	  
492	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
493	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
494	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
495	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
496	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
497	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
498	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
499	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
500	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
501	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
502	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
503	  \(k\). The \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is the \(k\)-scheme representing
504	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), supplied by
505	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
506	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
507	  locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of open
508	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes.
509	\end{definition}

625	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The carrier asserts that the
626	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ of
627	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} exists as a presheaf of types on
628	$(\Sch/k)^{op}$. The intended witness is the functor of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}
629	specialised to $X = C$ and post-composed with \'etale sheafification:
630	the assignment $T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)$
631	followed by the associated sheaf construction in the \'etale topology.

633	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.} The typeclass body unfolds to
634	\texttt{Nonempty ((Over (Spec (.of k)))${}^{op}$ \,$\rightsquigarrow$\, Type u)}.
635	\begin{itemize}
636	  \item \texttt{Functor}, \texttt{Nonempty}, \texttt{Over},
637	    \texttt{Spec}: \emph{exists} in Mathlib b80f227.
638	  \item The \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor as a specific
639	    Mathlib object: \emph{missing}. Sheafification of presheaves on a
640	    site exists (\texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.Plus},
641	    \texttt{Sheafify}), and the \'etale Grothendieck topology on
642	    \texttt{Scheme} is partially available, but the assembled
643	    \texttt{PicSharp} functor used downstream by
644	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is a project-internal target,
645	    not a Mathlib name.
646	  \item Bridge from \texttt{Pic(C ${}\times_k$ T) / $\pi_T^* \Pic(T)$}
647	    to a presheaf of types: \emph{needs-bridge}. Mathlib has
648	    \texttt{Module.Picard} / \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic} (the
649	    invertible-sheaf functor) but the relative quotient functor on
650	    $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ that the chapter cites is the project's bespoke
651	    construction in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}.
652	\end{itemize}

655	typeclass statement: \texttt{Nonempty} of a non-empty type is

660	captured by the \texttt{Prop}-valued typeclass. Closing the sorry to a

686	relative-effective-divisor functor $\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$ exists as a

688	$k$-scheme $T$ to the set of relative effective Cartier divisors on

698	    \emph{partial}. Mathlib has \texttt{Cartier} / \texttt{Divisor}
699	    machinery for divisors on a single scheme; the relative notion
700	    (flat over the base with invertible ideal sheaf) is partially
701	    spelled out in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Divisor} but not as a
702	    presheaf-valued functor on the slice category.
703	  \item Hilbert scheme / Quot scheme open subfunctor of relative
704	    effective divisors (Kleiman \S3 Thm.~\texttt{th:repDiv}):

721	\texttt{⟨⟨AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.divFunctor C⟩⟩}. Until then a
722	tautological closure leaves \texttt{divFunctor} semantically empty.

729	\subsection{Sorry 3 --- \texttt{instHasAbelMap} (L294)}
730	\label{subsec:sorry_has_abel_map}
731	
732	\paragraph{Location.} Lean file
733	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean},
734	line~294; instance
735	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.instHasAbelMap}.
736	
737	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The carrier asserts existence of
738	the Abel map
739	$A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ as a natural
740	transformation of presheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$, sending a relative
741	effective Cartier divisor $D \subseteq C \times_k T$ to its associated
742	invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) = \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$.
743	This is the substantive content of
744	\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
745	
746	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
747	\begin{itemize}
748	  \item Natural transformation \texttt{${\rightsquigarrow}$} of
749	    functors, \texttt{Nonempty}: \emph{exists}.
750	  \item Dual of an ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$ as an invertible
751	    sheaf for a relative effective Cartier divisor: \emph{partial}.
752	    Mathlib has dual modules and dual sheaves but the specific link
753	    "ideal of a relative effective Cartier divisor is invertible and
754	    its dual is the associated line bundle" is a project-internal
755	    bridge.
756	  \item Functoriality of the assignment under pullback (Kleiman \S3
757	    Lem.~\texttt{lm:ctn}): \emph{needs-bridge}. Pullback of relative
758	    effective divisors along base-change is standard for flat-over-
759	    base divisors; Mathlib's coverage of this for the scheme-theoretic
760	    relative setting is incomplete.
761	\end{itemize}
762	
763	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} None: the Abel map is a
764	construction at the level of line bundles and divisors, not a
765	consequence of Riemann--Roch. (Riemann--Roch tells us that the Abel
766	map for a fixed degree-$g$ pole-set is surjective onto $\Pic^g_{C/k}$;
767	this is a downstream consequence, not a prerequisite for defining the
768	map.)
769	
770	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} The component-wise definition
771	of $A_{C/k}(T)$ is: given $D \in \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)$, set
772	$A_{C/k}(T)(D) := [\mathcal{I}_D^{-1}]$ in
773	$\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$. Naturality follows from the pullback
774	formula for the dual ideal sheaf. The Lean encoding is a sequence of
775	three constructions: (a) extract the ideal sheaf of a relative
776	effective Cartier divisor, (b) dualise to get the line bundle,
777	(c) pass to the \'etale sheafified class. Each step is independent of
778	Riemann--Roch and depends only on Sorries~1 and~2 having committed to
779	a semantic witness for \texttt{picSharp} and \texttt{divFunctor}.
780	
781	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 2} (depends on Sorries~1
782	and~2; no Riemann--Roch substrate).
