784	\subsection{Sorry 4 --- \texttt{instHasSmoothProperQuotient} (L349)}
785	\label{subsec:sorry_smooth_proper_quotient}
786	
787	\paragraph{Location.} Lean file
788	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean},
789	line~349; instance constructor
790	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.instHasSmoothProperQuotient}
791	of the iter-199 \texttt{Prop}-valued typeclass
792	\texttt{HasSmoothProperQuotient} (L338-341). The associated theorem
793	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
794	(L377) extracts \texttt{HasSmoothProperQuotient.is\_representable}
795	and is itself axiom-clean.
796	
797	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The smooth-proper quotient lemma
798	of \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}: given a surjection
799	$\alpha : Z \to P$ of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ with $Z$
800	quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$ representable, and the first
801	projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper, the quotient $P$ is
802	representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme. The proof packages
803	the Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem for
804	flat-and-proper relations on quasi-projective schemes.
805	
806	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
807	\begin{itemize}
808	  \item \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable},
809	    \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Limits.pullback}:
810	    \emph{exists}.
811	  \item \texttt{Smooth}, \texttt{IsProper} typeclasses on scheme
812	    morphisms: \emph{exists}.
813	  \item EGA IV~8.11.5 "proper monomorphism is a closed immersion":
814	    \emph{missing}. Mathlib has closed immersions, monomorphisms of
815	    schemes, and properness, but the EGA combination lemma is not yet
816	    isolated as a named result.
817	  \item Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem
818	    (flat-and-proper equivalence relation on a quasi-projective
819	    scheme is effective, with quasi-projective quotient):
820	    \emph{missing}. This is the structural heart of the lemma and is
821	    not in Mathlib in any form.
822	  \item Coequaliser uniqueness in the category of \'etale sheaves:
823	    \emph{exists} (general categorical fact, uniqueness up to unique
824	    isomorphism of coequalisers in any category).
825	\end{itemize}
826	
827	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} None: the lemma is a
828	structural fact about quasi-projective schemes and \'etale sheaves,
829	independent of curves and Riemann--Roch. It does, however, sit on the
830	Mathlib gap "effective equivalence relations of schemes", which is a
831	substantial development in its own right (corresponds to roughly
832	Altman--Kleiman, "Compactifying the Picard scheme", and the
833	flat-and-proper descent of equivalence relations from EGA IV~17).
834	
835	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} Two routes are available:
836	\begin{enumerate}
837	  \item[(a)] Wait for the Mathlib gap "effective equivalence
838	    relations of quasi-projective schemes" to be filled and import
839	    the result. This is the cleanest long-term path but is outside
840	    the project's control timeline.
841	  \item[(b)] Formalise the Altman--Kleiman construction directly in
842	    the project as a sibling Lean file, gated on the EGA IV~8.11.5
843	    lemma (proper monomorphism is a closed immersion) which can be
844	    extracted from existing Mathlib infrastructure in a focused
845	    sub-task.
846	\end{enumerate}
847	Route~(b) is the recommended iter-199+ attempt: the EGA IV~8.11.5
848	extraction is bounded and the remaining structural construction
849	(graph of the equivalence relation inside the Hilbert scheme of $Z$,
850	descent to a closed subscheme of $\Hilb_{Z/k}$) is intrinsic.
851	
852	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 2} (substantial Mathlib
853	gap; no Riemann--Roch substrate; recommended first attempt under the
854	carrier-soundness probe).

856	\subsection{Sorry 5 --- \texttt{instHasPicScheme} (L236)}
857	\label{subsec:sorry_has_pic_scheme}
858	
859	\paragraph{Location.} Lean file
860	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean},
861	line~236; instance
862	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.instHasPicScheme}.
863	
864	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The carrier asserts that there
865	exists an object $X \in \mathrm{Over}(\Spec k)$ together with a
866	\texttt{RepresentableBy} witness for $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$. In
867	other words: the Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ exists. The semantic
868	content is the conclusion of Kleiman \S4 Thm.~\texttt{th:main}
869	specialised by Cor.~\texttt{cor:algsch} to $S = \Spec k$ and $X = C$
870	a smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
871	
872	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
873	\begin{itemize}
874	  \item \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Nonempty},
875	    \texttt{Over}: \emph{exists}.
876	  \item \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1},
877	    \texttt{IsProper}, \texttt{GeometricallyIntegral}: \emph{exists}
878	    as typeclasses, but the rich theory needed to actually produce a
879	    representing scheme is largely missing.
880	  \item The FGA representability machinery itself (the four-step
881	    construction of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, namely
882	    Hilbert polynomial stratification, $m$-regularity bound, Abel
883	    map factorisation, smooth-proper quotient): \emph{missing}.
884	  \item Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity / $m$-regularity for
885	    cohomologically twist-vanishing sheaves: \emph{missing}.
886	  \item Riemann--Roch for line bundles on a smooth proper curve over
887	    a field (used in the $m$-regularity step to bound twists by the
888	    degree): \emph{missing} (Route~C).
889	\end{itemize}
890	
891	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} \textbf{Required}. The
892	$m$-regularity bound (Kleiman \S4 Step~2 of the proof of
893	Thm.~\texttt{th:main}) is the point at which the structural argument
894	meets Riemann--Roch: for a curve $C/k$ of genus $g$ and a line
895	bundle $\mathcal{L}$ of degree $d$, $H^1(C, \mathcal{L}(n)) = 0$ for
896	$n \geq m$ depends on $m \geq 2g - 1 - d + \deg \mathcal{O}_C(1)$, which
897	is the Serre vanishing consequence of Riemann--Roch. Specifically the
898	required result is the curve specialisation of
899	Hartshorne~III.5.2 (Serre vanishing) combined with IV.1.3
900	(Riemann--Roch for curves). Without Riemann--Roch, the $m$-regularity
901	step cannot be discharged and the FGA construction does not
902	terminate.
903	
904	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} Conditional on the Route~C
905	re-engagement that the project STRATEGY.md flags as
906	substrate-blocked: once the Riemann--Roch arc lands, the $m$-regularity
907	step can be discharged via Hartshorne III.5.2 / IV.1.3, then the
908	remaining three steps (stratification, Abel-map factorisation,
909	smooth-proper quotient via Sorry~4) assemble into the existence
910	witness. Until then this sorry is the load-bearing blocker on the
911	Pic scheme arm of Route~A.
912	
913	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 3} (requires the Route~C
914	substrate; load-bearing on the entire A.2.c representability claim).

1025	\subsection{Closure-order summary}
1026	\label{subsec:fga_pic_closure_order_summary}
1027	
1028	The seven sorries partition into three ranks:
1029	\begin{itemize}
1030	  \item \textbf{Rank 1} (independent, closeable from existing
1031	    Mathlib alone): none. Every sorry has either a sibling-chapter
1032	    dependency, a Mathlib gap, or a Route~C substrate dependency.
1033	  \item \textbf{Rank 2} (depends on sibling chapters or a
1034	    non-Riemann--Roch Mathlib gap; closeable in iter-199+ under the
1035	    carrier-soundness probe): Sorries~1, 2, 3, and~4.
1036	    \begin{itemize}
1037	      \item Sorries~1 and~2 are gated on the A.1.c
1038	        (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) and A.2.b
1039	        (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}) sibling chapters
1040	        committing to a specific \texttt{PicSharp} and
1041	        \texttt{divFunctor} pin.
1042	      \item Sorry~3 (Abel map) is gated on Sorries~1 and~2.
1043	      \item Sorry~4 (smooth-proper quotient) is the substantive
1044	        structural lemma; closeable independently of the other six
1045	        by formalising the EGA IV~8.11.5 step and the Altman--Kleiman
1046	        descent.
1047	    \end{itemize}
1048	  \item \textbf{Rank 3} (requires the Route~C substrate; blocked
1049	    until the Riemann--Roch arc lands): Sorries~5, 6, and~7.
1050	    \begin{itemize}
1051	      \item Sorry~5 (Picard scheme existence) is the load-bearing
1052	        Route~C-blocked carrier via the $m$-regularity / Serre
1053	        vanishing dependency.
1054	      \item Sorry~6 (representability witness) inherits the Route~C
1055	        dependency of Sorry~5.
1056	      \item Sorry~7 (group-scheme structure) is Route~C-independent
1057	        in itself but depends on Sorry~6.
1058	    \end{itemize}
1059	\end{itemize}
1060	
1061	\medskip
1062	
1063	\textbf{Recommended first attempt under the carrier-soundness
1064	probe.} Sorry~4 (the \texttt{instHasSmoothProperQuotient}
1065	\texttt{⟨sorry⟩}) is the highest-value rank-2 target: it is a
1066	structural lemma whose discharge does not depend on any sibling
1067	chapter committing first, and after the iter-199 carrier-soundness
1068	refactor it isolates the only mathematical obligation of
1069	\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} into a single instance
1070	constructor. The EGA IV~8.11.5 sub-step is bounded, and the
1071	Altman--Kleiman descent has a known proof sketch in
1072	\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} of this chapter.

3279	  The point is that the finality hypothesis holds \emph{unconditionally}, for every
3280	  morphism of schemes \(f\). The preimage functor \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}
3281	  : \mathtt{Opens}\,X \to \mathtt{Opens}\,Y\) is a frame homomorphism: it preserves
3282	  arbitrary unions and finite intersections, hence preserves all finite limits. A
3283	  functor between small categories that preserves finite limits is representably flat,
3284	  so \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\) gives \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}).\mathtt{Final}\)
3285	  with no hypothesis on \(f\) whatsoever. (There is no need to pass to affine charts:
3286	  finality is not a special feature of open immersions but a general property of
3287	  preimage-on-opens.)
3288	
3289	  Consequently the instance \(\mathtt{instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\) fires
3290	  directly, and \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The asserted
3291	  isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the bundled iso
3292	  \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\,f\) of this comparison.
3293	\end{proof}

3401	presheaf monoidal unit; \(F = \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'\) the presheaf pullback;
3402	\(\eta\,F : F.\mathrm{obj}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}} \to \mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}\) its oplax unit
3403	comparison; and \(\varepsilon(G)\) the lax unit comparison of a lax monoidal functor \(G\). The
3404	isomorphisms \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,f\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and \(\mathtt{sheafifyUnitIso}\) are
3405	the file's comparison morphisms relating the sheaf-level pullback value to the sheafification of
3406	the presheaf-level pullback. Write
3407	\(\mathtt{sheafCounit}_\bullet = (\mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}\,(\mathbf{1}_{\bullet.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{val}})).\mathrm{counit}\).

3409	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3410	[Presheaf-side unit mate identity]
3411	  \label{lem:presheaf_unit_comp_map_eta}
3412	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.presheafUnit_comp_map_eta}
3413	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal, lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}
3414	  At the presheaf level the pullback--pushforward adjunction unit at the monoidal unit,
3415	  post-composed with the pushforward of the oplax unit comparison, recovers the lax unit:
3416	  \[
3417	    \mathtt{presheafAdj}.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}
3418	      \;\mathbin{;}\;
3419	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi').\mathrm{map}\,(\eta\,F)
3420	      \;=\; \varepsilon(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi'),
3421	  \]
3422	  where \(\mathtt{presheafAdj} = \mathtt{pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi'\) is the
3423	  presheaf pullback--pushforward adjunction and \(F = \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'\).
3424	\end{lemma}
3425	
3426	\begin{proof}
3427	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal, lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}
3428	  \leanok
3429	  This is Mathlib's general adjunction-mate identity
3430	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.unit\_app\_unit\_comp\_map\_}\eta\) instantiated at the presheaf
3431	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \(\mathtt{pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi'\). That
3432	  identity holds for any adjunction whose left adjoint is oplax monoidal and whose right adjoint
3433	  is lax monoidal in a mate-compatible way. The compatible pair is
3434	  \(\mathtt{presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal}\)
3435	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}) and \(\mathtt{presheafPullbackOplaxMonoidal}\)
3436	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}), so the
3437	  mate identity applies to the concrete adjunction and yields the stated equation.
3438	\end{proof}

3474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3475	[The unit square]
3476	  \label{lem:eta_bridge_unit_square}
3477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackEtaUnitSquare}
3478	  \uses{lem:presheaf_unit_comp_map_eta, lem:comp_homequiv_factor_sheafify_pullback,
3479	        lem:leftadjointuniq_app_unit_eta, lem:epsilon_presheaf_to_sheaf_unit,
3480	        lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
3481	  The unit square commutes:
3482	  \[
3483	    (\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,f\,\mathcal{O}_X).\mathrm{inv} \;\mathbin{;}\;
3484	      a_Y.\mathrm{map}\,(\eta\,F) \;\mathbin{;}\;
3485	      \mathtt{sheafifyUnitIso}.\mathrm{hom}
3486	      \;=\; \mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\,\varphi.
3487	  \]
3488	\end{lemma}
3489	
3490	\begin{proof}
3491	  \uses{lem:presheaf_unit_comp_map_eta, lem:comp_homequiv_factor_sheafify_pullback,
3492	        lem:leftadjointuniq_app_unit_eta, lem:epsilon_presheaf_to_sheaf_unit}
3493	  \leanok
3494	  Transpose the square across the \emph{sheaf} pullback--pushforward adjunction
3495	  \(\mathtt{pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi\) by applying
3496	  \(\mathtt{homEquiv}.\mathrm{injective}\) and then \(\mathtt{homEquiv\_unit}\),
3497	  \(\mathtt{homEquiv\_counit}\). Since
3498	  \(\mathtt{homEquiv}(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\,\varphi) =
3499	  \mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) (the mate identity for the structure-sheaf unit
3500	  comparison), the square reduces to the concrete pushforward-side identity
3501	  \begin{equation}
3502	    \mathtt{sheafAdj}.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathcal{O}_X \;\mathbin{;}\;
3503	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{map}\,(m)
3504	      \;=\; \mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi,
3505	    \tag{$\ast\ast$}
3506	  \end{equation}
3507	  where \(m\) is the four-fold composite
3508	  \[
3509	    m \;=\;
3510	      (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{map}\,(\mathtt{sheafCounit}_X.\mathrm{inv})
3511	      \;\mathbin{;}\;
3512	      (\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{hom}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}
3513	      \;\mathbin{;}\;
3514	      a_Y.\mathrm{map}\,(\eta\,F)
3515	      \;\mathbin{;}\;
3516	      \mathtt{sheafCounit}_Y.\mathrm{hom},
3517	  \]
3518	  \(\mathtt{sheafAdj} = \mathtt{pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi\), and
3519	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\,\varphi = \mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\,A\,B\) is
3520	  the canonical comparison of the two composite adjunctions
3521	  \begin{itemize}
3522	    \item \(A\) with left adjoint \(a_X \circ \mathtt{pullback}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\,\varphi\),
3523	      namely \(A = (\mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}\,(\mathbf{1}_X)).\mathrm{comp}\,
3524	      (\mathtt{pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\,\varphi)\);
3525	    \item \(B\) with left adjoint \(F \circ a_Y\), namely
3526	      \(B = (\mathtt{pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi').\mathrm{comp}\,
3527	      (\mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}\,(\mathbf{1}_Y))\).
3528	  \end{itemize}
3529	  We prove $(\ast\ast)$ in seven steps.
3530	
3531	  \begin{enumerate}
3532	    \item \textbf{Distribute.} By functoriality of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\)
3533	      (\(\mathtt{Functor.map\_comp}\)), expand
3534	      \((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{map}\,(m)\) over the four factors of \(m\), so
3535	      that the left side of $(\ast\ast)$ becomes
3536	      \(\mathtt{sheafAdj}.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) followed by the four
3537	      pushed-forward factors.
3538	    \item \textbf{Unit naturality.} Pull the leading factor
3539	      \((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{map}\bigl((\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{map}\,(\mathtt{sheafCounit}_X.\mathrm{inv})\bigr)\)
3540	      past the unit using the naturality square \(\mathtt{Adjunction.unit\_naturality}\) of
3541	      \(\mathtt{sheafAdj}\). This rewrites the head as
3542	      \[
3543	        \mathtt{sheafCounit}_X.\mathrm{inv} \;\mathbin{;}\;
3544	          \mathtt{sheafAdj}.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,(a_X.\mathrm{obj}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}})
3545	          \;\mathbin{;}\;
3546	          (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{map}\bigl(
3547	            (\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{hom}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}\bigr)
3548	          \;\mathbin{;}\;\cdots,
3549	      \]
3550	      leaving the genuinely adjunction-theoretic head
3551	      \(\mathtt{sheafAdj}.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,(a_X.\mathrm{obj}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}})
3552	      \mathbin{;} (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{map}\,(g)\) with
3553	      \(g = (\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{hom}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}\).
3554	    \item \textbf{Composite-adjunction homEquiv factorisation}
3555	      (\cref{lem:comp_homequiv_factor_sheafify_pullback}). The head of step~2 is
3556	      \((\mathtt{sheafAdj}_X.\mathrm{homEquiv})^{-1}\bigl(A.\mathrm{homEquiv}\,g\bigr)\),
3557	      where \(A.\mathrm{homEquiv}\) is the hom-set bijection of the composite adjunction \(A\).
3558	    \item \textbf{leftAdjointUniq transport}
3559	      (\cref{lem:leftadjointuniq_app_unit_eta}). Applying
3560	      \(\mathtt{Adjunction.homEquiv\_leftAdjointUniq\_hom\_app}\,A\,B\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}\)
3561	      rewrites \(A.\mathrm{homEquiv}\,g = B.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}\),
3562	      and expanding \(B.\mathrm{unit}\) by \(\mathtt{Adjunction.comp\_unit\_app}\) on \(B\) gives
3563	      \[
3564	        B.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}
3565	          \;=\;
3566	          \mathtt{presheafAdj}.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}
3567	          \;\mathbin{;}\;
3568	          (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi').\mathrm{map}\bigl(\mathtt{toSheafify}_Y(F.\mathrm{obj}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}})\bigr).
3569	      \]
3570	    \item \textbf{Composite-adjunction unit expansion.} The expansion of \(B.\mathrm{unit}\) used in step~4
3571	      follows from \(\mathtt{Adjunction.comp\_unit\_app}\) applied to the composite
3572	      \(B = (\mathtt{pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi').\mathrm{comp}\,
3573	      (\mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}\,(\mathbf{1}_Y))\): the unit of a composite adjunction
3574	      is the pasting of the two factor units.
3575	    \item \textbf{Presheaf-side mate identity}
3576	      (\cref{lem:presheaf_unit_comp_map_eta}). The oplax unit comparison \(\eta\,F\) enters
3577	      through the factor \(a_Y.\mathrm{map}\,(\eta\,F)\) of \(m\); the surrounding
3578	      sheafification counits and the \(\mathtt{toSheafify}_Y\) of step~4 cancel through
3579	      \(\mathtt{sheafCounit}_Y\), exposing the presheaf composite
3580	      \(\mathtt{presheafAdj}.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}} \mathbin{;}
3581	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi').\mathrm{map}\,(\eta\,F)\). By
3582	      \cref{lem:presheaf_unit_comp_map_eta} this equals the presheaf-level lax unit
3583	      \(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{pre}} = \varepsilon(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi')\).
3584	    \item \textbf{$\varepsilon$ reconciliation}
3585	      (\cref{lem:epsilon_presheaf_to_sheaf_unit}). Finally identify the presheaf-level
3586	      \(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{pre}}\) with the sheaf-level structure-sheaf comparison
3587	      \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) at the \((-).\mathrm{val}\)
3588	      (underlying-presheaf) level --- both act on sections as \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}.\mathrm{app}\,X\)
3589	      --- using the pushforward \(\leftrightarrow\) sheafification compatibility that defines the two
3590	      adjunctions. This completes $(\ast\ast)$, hence the unit square.
3591	  \end{enumerate}
3592	\end{proof}

3594	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3595	[Composite-adjunction homEquiv factorisation]
3596	  \label{lem:comp_homequiv_factor_sheafify_pullback}
3597	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.compHomEquivFactor}
3598	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
3599	  Let \(\mathtt{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\) and \(\mathtt{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\) be composable
3600	  adjunctions and \(A = \mathtt{adj}_1.\mathrm{comp}\,\mathtt{adj}_2 : L_1 \circ L_2 \dashv
3601	  R_2 \circ R_1\) their composite. Then the hom-set bijection of the composite factors as the
3602	  composite of the two factor bijections: for every \(g\),
3603	  \[
3604	    A.\mathrm{homEquiv}\,g
3605	      \;=\;
3606	    \mathtt{adj}_1.\mathrm{homEquiv}\bigl(\mathtt{adj}_2.\mathrm{homEquiv}\,g\bigr).
3607	  \]
3608	  Consequently, for the composite adjunction \(A\) of the unit square (left adjoint
3609	  \(a_X \circ \mathtt{pullback}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\,\varphi\)), the head of step~2 of
3610	  \cref{lem:eta_bridge_unit_square} equals
3611	  \((\mathtt{sheafAdj}_X.\mathrm{homEquiv})^{-1}\bigl(A.\mathrm{homEquiv}\,g\bigr)\) with
3612	  \(g = (\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{hom}.\mathrm{app}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}\).
3613	\end{lemma}
3614	
3615	\begin{proof}
3616	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
3617	  \leanok
3618	  The composite adjunction \(\mathtt{Adjunction.comp}\) is defined so that its unit is the
3619	  pasting \(\mathtt{adj}_1.\mathrm{unit} \mathbin{;} R_1(\mathtt{adj}_2.\mathrm{unit})_{L_1(-)}\)
3620	  and its counit dually; equivalently its hom-set bijection \(A.\mathrm{homEquiv}\) is the
3621	  composite of the two factor bijections in the order \(\mathtt{adj}_2.\mathrm{homEquiv}\) then
3622	  \(\mathtt{adj}_1.\mathrm{homEquiv}\). This is the standard factorisation
3623	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.comp\_homEquiv}\) (the naturality of \(\mathtt{homEquiv}\) under
3624	  composition of adjunctions): a morphism \(L_1 L_2\,c \to d\) is transposed first across
3625	  \(\mathtt{adj}_1\) to \(L_2\,c \to R_1\,d\) and then across \(\mathtt{adj}_2\) to
3626	  \(c \to R_2 R_1\,d\). Applying this with \(\mathtt{adj}_1 =
3627	  \mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}\,(\mathbf{1}_X)\), \(\mathtt{adj}_2 =
3628	  \mathtt{pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\,\varphi\) and transposing back along
3629	  \(\mathtt{adj}_1\) (i.e.\ applying \((\mathtt{sheafAdj}_X.\mathrm{homEquiv})^{-1}\)) gives the
3630	  stated rewrite of the head of step~2.
3631	\end{proof}

3636	[The sheafify-then-pullback comparison is the left-adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism]
3637	  \label{lem:sheafification_comp_pullback_eq_leftadjointuniq}
3638	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCompPullback_eq_leftAdjointUniq}
3639	  Write \(A\) for the composite adjunction obtained by following the sheafification adjunction
3640	  on \(X\) with the sheaf-level pullback--pushforward adjunction of \(\varphi\), namely
3641	  \(A = (\mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}\,(\mathbf{1}_X)).\mathrm{comp}\,
3642	  (\mathtt{pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\,\varphi)\), whose left adjoint is
3643	  \(a_X \circ \mathtt{pullback}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\,\varphi\); and write \(B\) for the composite
3644	  adjunction obtained by following the presheaf-level pullback--pushforward adjunction of
3645	  \(\varphi'\) with the sheafification adjunction on \(Y\), namely
3646	  \(B = (\mathtt{pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi').\mathrm{comp}\,
3647	  (\mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}\,(\mathbf{1}_Y))\), whose left adjoint is \(F \circ a_Y\).
3648	  These two left adjoints are canonically isomorphic, and the canonical ``sheafify-then-pullback''
3649	  comparison agrees on the nose with the left-adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism:
3650	  \[
3651	    \mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\,\varphi
3652	      \;=\;

3654	  \]

3657	\begin{proof}
3658	
3659	\leanok

3661	  pushing forward along \(\varphi\) and then forgetting to presheaves of modules on \(X\) is, on
3662	  the nose, the same as forgetting to presheaves on \(Y\) and then pushing forward along
3663	  \(\varphi'\) --- the pushforward commutes with the forgetful functor to presheaves by
3664	  definition. Hence \(A\) and \(B\) are two adjunctions sharing the same right adjoint, so the
3665	  left-adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\,A\,B\) between their

3668	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\,A\,B\) produces the same morphism --- both sides are the
3669	  composite isomorphism assembled from the two factor adjunctions in the same order. The equality
3670	  therefore holds by reflexivity.

3714	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3715	[$\varepsilon$ reconciliation: presheaf unit to sheaf unit on underlying presheaves]
3716	  \label{lem:epsilon_presheaf_to_sheaf_unit}
3717	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.epsilonPresheafToSheafUnit}
3718	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal, lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
3719	  The presheaf-level lax-monoidal unit comparison
3720	  \(\varepsilon(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi')\) and the sheaf-level structure-sheaf unit
3721	  comparison \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) agree as morphisms of
3722	  \emph{underlying presheaves} of modules: after applying the forgetful functor
3723	  \((-).\mathrm{val}\) that sends a sheaf of modules to its underlying presheaf, their
3724	  components coincide. Concretely, on every section both maps act as the ring map
3725	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}.\mathrm{app}\) underlying \(\varphi\):
3726	  \[
3727	    (\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi).\mathrm{val}.\mathrm{app}\,X\,a
3728	      \;=\; \varphi.\mathrm{hom}.\mathrm{app}\,X\,a,
3729	  \]
3730	  and the presheaf unit comparison \(\varepsilon(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi')\) takes the
3731	  same value on sections. The asserted identity is the equality of these two presheaf-level
3732	  morphisms (the \((-).\mathrm{val}\) images), \emph{not} a sheaf-level lax-monoidal-unit
3733	  equation.
3734	
3735	  The exact form of the identity is the \((-).\mathrm{val}\)-level (underlying-presheaf)
3736	  reconciliation, because the sheaf-level pushforward carries no lax monoidal structure in this
3737	  setup: the presheaf lax unit
3738	  \(\varepsilon(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi')\) is the one that exists, and
3739	  it is reconciled with the sheaf-level \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) through
3740	  the sheafification \(\leftrightarrow\) pushforward compatibility.
3741	\end{lemma}
3742	
3743	\begin{proof}
3744	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal, lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
3745	  \leanok
3746	  Equality of presheaf morphisms is checked sectionwise. Over each open both presheaf-level
3747	  maps are the single ring map \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}.\mathrm{app}\,X\): the presheaf lax unit
3748	  of a pushforward (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}) is the structure-map
3749	  comparison \(\mathcal{O} \to \varphi_*\mathcal{O}\), and the underlying presheaf component of
3750	  \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) is the same comparison by construction --- its
3751	  value on a section is \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}.\mathrm{app}\,X\) (the same comparison whose
3752	  composition coherence is \cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}). Since the two presheaf
3753	  morphisms agree on every section, they are equal. Tracing the sheafification reconciliation
3754	  backwards then identifies this presheaf-level identity with the step~6 presheaf head
3755	  \(\varepsilon(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi')\) of \cref{lem:eta_bridge_unit_square}, closing
3756	  $(\ast\ast)$.
3757	\end{proof}

3759	% NOTE: D3' is the SOLE genuinely-new sub-step of the loc-triv route. It is the
3760	% tensorator analog of the already-built, axiom-clean unit coherence
3761	% lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, established by the same mate calculus
3762	% (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.comp_delta + conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv). Feasibility is

4116	\]
4117	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
4118	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
4119	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
4120	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
4121	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
4122	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
4123	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
4124	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
4125	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).
4126	
4127	\begin{lemma}
4128	
4129	\leanok
4130	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible objects (corollary of the unconditional
4131	associator)]
4132	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
4133	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}
4134	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
4135	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
4136	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
4137	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
4138	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
4139	  \[
4140	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
4141	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
4142	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
4143	  \]
4144	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
4145	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
4146	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
4147	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
4148	  coherence is ever required.
4149	\end{lemma}
4150	
4151	\begin{proof}
4152	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
4153	  \leanok
4154	  Immediate specialisation of the \emph{unconditional} associator
4155	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to the invertible arguments \(M, N, P\); no
4156	  hypothesis on the arguments is used. (The earlier route argued via ``invertible
4157	  \(\Rightarrow\) locally free \(\Rightarrow\) sectionwise flat'' to feed the
4158	  flat-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}; that step is \emph{false}
4159	  --- local freeness does not give global sectionwise flatness \(M(U)\) over
4160	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) for a non-affine open \(U\) --- and is no longer needed, since
4161	  the general associator is itself unconditional, its single open left-whiskering
4162	  obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} being closed via the d.2
4163	  stalk--tensor commutation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.)
4164	\end{proof}

4177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4178	  % "\begin{definition}
4179	  % \label{definition-pic}

4186	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}:
4187	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) is the abelian group of isomorphism classes of
4188	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, with addition the tensor product.}
4189	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. Consider the subtype of \(\otimes\)-invertible objects
4190	  \[
4191	    \mathtt{Invertible}\,X
4192	    \;:=\;
4193	    \bigl\{\, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
4194	              \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
4195	  \]
4196	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and equip it with the setoid whose
4197	  relation is ``there exists an isomorphism'',
4198	  \(M \sim M' :\equiv \mathrm{Nonempty}(M \cong M')\) (a proposition-valued
4199	  equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric and transitive because isomorphisms
4200	  compose and invert). The \emph{Picard group carrier}
4201	  \[
4202	    \Pic X \;:=\; \mathtt{Invertible}\,X \,/\!\sim
4203	  \]

4205	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. We write \([M] \in \Pic X\) for the class of an
4206	  invertible \(M\). This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
4207	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\), carried on \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) rather than on
4208	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\); on a scheme the two predicates agree (Stacks tag
4209	  0B8M), but only the invertibility form carries the inverse witness existentially.

4357	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}

4360	  with isomorphisms in place of equalities. Chain the unitor
4361	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the two hypotheses, the associator
4362	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible} and the braiding
4363	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}:

4365	    N

4367	    \;\cong\; N \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N')
4368	    \;\cong\; (N \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N'
4369	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X N'

4422	  abelian group, with
4423	  \[
4424	    [M] \cdot [M'] := [M \otimes_X M'],
4425	    \qquad
4426	    1 := [\mathcal{O}_X],

4432	\begin{proof}
4433	  \uses{def:pic_carrier, lem:isinvertible_tensor, lem:isinvertible_unit,
4434	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
4435	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
4436	  Each group-operation datum and each axiom is supplied by a single
4437	  existence-of-isomorphism, read as an equality of classes in \(\Pic X\).
4438	
4439	  \emph{The operations are well-defined.} The product \([M] \cdot [M'] := [M
4440	  \otimes_X M']\) lands in \(\Pic X\) because \(\otimes_X\) preserves invertibility
4441	  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_tensor}), and it is independent of representatives by the
4442	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
4443	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}): an isomorphism in either
4444	  argument tensors to an isomorphism, hence an equality of classes. The unit
4445	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) lies in \(\Pic X\) by \cref{lem:isinvertible_unit}. The
4446	  inverse \([M] \mapsto [N]\), \(N\) any membership witness of
4447	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\), is well-defined by
4448	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef} --- the witness is determined up to
4449	  isomorphism, so its class depends only on \([M]\). Crucially, \emph{no inverse
4450	  object is constructed}: the inverse is read off the existential carried by
4451	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\).
4452	
4453	  \emph{The axioms.} Each axiom is the image, under passage to iso-classes, of one
4454	  coherence isomorphism of \(\otimes_X\):
4455	  \[
4456	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle \lambda \rangle,
4457	    \quad
4458	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle \rho \rangle,
4459	    \quad
4460	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle \alpha \rangle,
4461	    \quad
4462	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle \beta \rangle,
4463	    \quad
4464	    \mathtt{mul\_left\_inv} \leftarrow \langle \varepsilon \rangle,
4465	  \]
4466	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity
4467	  is the invertible-case associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible},
4468	  commutativity is the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, and the left-inverse
4469	  law \([N] \cdot [M] = [N \otimes_X M] = [\mathcal{O}_X] = 1\) is the witness
4470	  isomorphism \(\varepsilon : N \otimes_X M \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) itself (obtained
4471	  from \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the braiding). Each axiom asserts
4472	  only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so no pentagon, triangle, or
4473	  hexagon coherence is invoked; commutativity makes the left-inverse law suffice
4474	  for a two-sided inverse.
4475	
4476	  This is the invertibility-carrier analogue of
4477	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, differing in exactly one respect: the
4478	  left-inverse field is discharged by the predicate's own membership witness rather
4479	  than by the manufactured dual of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The
4480	  whole dual-infrastructure obligation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} is
4481	  thereby off this group's critical path.
4482	\end{proof}

4823	A structural point governs the whole construction. The naive rule
4824	\(U \mapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) is \emph{contravariant} in the
4825	restriction maps of \(M\) (a restriction \(M(U) \to M(V)\) for \(V \subseteq U\)
4826	induces precomposition \(\Hom(M(V), N(V)) \to \Hom(M(U), \cdot)\) in the wrong
4827	direction), so it is \emph{not} a presheaf in the covariant sense and cannot be
4828	sheafified like the tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The remedy is
4829	the \emph{slice} formula \(U \mapsto (M|_U \to N|_U)\) --- the module of morphisms of
4830	\emph{restricted} modules over the restricted ring --- whose presheaf functoriality
4831	(restrict a global-over-\(U\) morphism to a global-over-\(V\) morphism, for
4832	\(V \subseteq U\)) \emph{is} covariant. Constructing that slice presheaf, and
4833	proving it satisfies the sheaf condition, is the substance of the build.

4851	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
4852	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
4853	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
4854	  (tag area 01CM): the section ring acts on a morphism of restricted modules by
4855	  post-composition with multiplication by the section.}
4856	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a base category \(\mathcal{C}\)
4857	  that has a terminal object \(T\), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\).
4858	  The abelian group of morphisms \(M \longrightarrow N\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
4859	  carries a canonical \(R(T)\)-module structure --- the \emph{morphism module}
4860	  \(\mathtt{homModule}\,M\,N\). A global scalar \(f \in R(T)\) acts on a morphism
4861	  \(\varphi : M \to N\) by post-composition with multiplication by \(f\),
4862	  \[
4863	    f \cdot \varphi \;:=\; m_f^{N} \circ \varphi,
4864	  \]
4865	  where \(m_f^{N} : N \to N\) is the \emph{multiplication-by-\(f\)} endomorphism of
4866	  \(N\): the endomorphism that, on each object \(U \in \mathcal{C}\), is multiplication
4867	  by the restriction \(f|_U \in R(U)\) of \(f\) along the unique morphism \(U \to T\)
4868	  to the terminal object. The module axioms hold because the assignment
4869	  \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\) is a ring homomorphism \(R(T) \to \mathrm{End}(N)\) into the
4870	  endomorphism ring of \(N\) --- a product \(f \cdot g\) goes to the \emph{composite}
4871	  \(m_f^{N} \circ m_g^{N}\) of the two multiplication endomorphisms, the order in which
4872	  the factors compose being reversed by the fact that the scalar acts by
4873	  \emph{post}-composition --- and composition of morphisms is biadditive in the
4874	  preadditive category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). Distributivity and the
4875	  associativity of the action then follow from bilinearity of composition together with
4876	  the homomorphism property of \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\). This is the genuinely new core of
4877	  the construction: Mathlib supplies the \emph{fixed}-ring internal hom
4878	  \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\) over a single base ring, but nothing equipping the
4879	  morphism group with the module structure for the \emph{varying} structure presheaf at
4880	  the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
4881	\end{definition}
4882	
4883	\begin{definition}
4884	
4885	\leanok
4886	[Slice specialisation of the morphism module]
4887	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
4888	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHomObjModule}
4889	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
4890	  Specialise \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} to a slice category. For an object
4891	  (open) \(U\) of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\), restricting along the forgetful functor
4892	  \(\mathtt{Over.forget}\,U : \mathcal{C}/U \to \mathcal{C}\) --- the open-immersion
4893	  restriction \(M \mapsto M|_U\) --- carries \(M, N\) to presheaves of modules
4894	  \(M|_U, N|_U\) over the restricted structure ring on the slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\). The
4895	  slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\) has the terminal object \(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(\mathrm{id}_U)\),
4896	  at which the restricted ring takes the value \(R(U)\); hence
4897	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} makes the morphism group
4898	  \(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\) an \(R(U)\)-module, the
4899	  \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\). This \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) is exactly
4900	  the value \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
4901	\end{definition}

4903	\begin{definition}
4904	
4905	\leanok
4906	[Presheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
4907	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
4908	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHom}
4909	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:presheaf_internal_hom_value,
4910	        def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
4911	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
4912	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
4913	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3500--L3524).
4914	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4915	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
4916	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
4917	  % Consider the rule
4918	  % $$
4919	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
4920	  % $$
4921	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
4922	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
4923	  % abelian groups. In addition, given an element
4924	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
4925	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
4926	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
4927	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
4928	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
4929	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
4930	  % We will denote this sheaf
4931	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$.
4932	  % There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
4933	  % $$
4934	  % \mathcal{F}
4935	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4936	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
4937	  % \longrightarrow
4938	  % \mathcal{G}.
4939	  % $$"
4940	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
4941	  (tag area 01CM).}
4942	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on \(\Opens X\) and let
4943	  \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). The \emph{presheaf internal hom}
4944	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules whose value on an open
4945	  \(U\) is the module of morphisms of restricted modules
4946	  \[
4947	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)
4948	      \;:=\;
4949	    \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\;\bigl(R(U)\bigr)\;\bigl(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\bigr),
4950	  \]
4951	  the \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear morphisms \(M|_U \to N|_U\) of presheaves of
4952	  modules on \(U\), formed via the open-immersion restriction \(U \hookrightarrow X\).
4953	  This value module is the slice specialisation
4954	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value} of the morphism module
4955	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}.
4956	
4957	  Explicitly, \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules assembled from
4958	  three pieces. \emph{(a) Value modules.} On each open \(U\) the value is the
4959	  \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} /
4960	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}. \emph{(b) Restriction maps.} For an
4961	  inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) the restriction map
4962	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \to \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V)\) is the further-restriction map
4963	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}, \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|_V\), which
4964	  is additive and semilinear over the ring restriction \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\)
4965	  induced by \(g : V \to U\) --- the compatibility datum that the presheaf-of-modules
4966	  assembly (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.mk}\), via \(\mathtt{ofPresheaf}\)) consumes.
4967	  \emph{(c) Functoriality.} Restriction along the identity \(U \to U\) is the identity
4968	  map, and restriction along a composite \(V \xrightarrow{g} U \xrightarrow{h} W\) of
4969	  inclusions \(V \subseteq U \subseteq W\) (the morphism \(g : V \to U\) followed by
4970	  \(h : U \to W\) in \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\)) is the composite of the two
4971	  restriction maps \(\mathcal{H}om(M,N)(W) \to \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(U) \to
4972	  \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(V)\); both identity and composition are inherited from the
4973	  functoriality of further restriction, since \(\mathtt{Over.map}\) is a functor. These three pieces
4974	  make \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) a genuine, covariant presheaf of \(R\)-modules; this
4975	  assembled presheaf is the construction target \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}\).
4976	
4977	  The slice formula is forced by contravariance: the pointwise rule
4978	  \(U \mapsto \Hom_{R(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) varies \emph{against} the restriction maps of
4979	  \(M\) and so is not a covariant presheaf, whereas the module of morphisms of
4980	  \emph{restricted} objects \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricts covariantly. Thus, unlike the
4981	  tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} --- which is covariant in the
4982	  restriction maps and sheafifies directly --- the internal hom must be built through
4983	  the slice presentation, and there is no presheaf-level dual to sheafify naively.
4984	\end{definition}

4986	\begin{lemma}
4987	
4988	\leanok
4989	[Restriction map of the presheaf internal hom]
4990	  \label{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
4991	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.restrictionMap}
4992	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value, def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
4993	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
4994	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
4995	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3508).
4996	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4997	  % "Consider the rule
4998	  % $$
4999	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
5000	  % $$
5001	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
5002	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
5003	  % abelian groups."
5004	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
5005	  (tag area 01CM): the rule \(U \mapsto \Hom(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)\), with its
5006	  further-restriction maps, is a (pre)sheaf of abelian groups.}
5007	  Let \(g : V \to U\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\) (an inclusion
5008	  \(V \subseteq U\) of opens), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). There
5009	  is a restriction map
5010	  \[
5011	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V),
5012	    \qquad \varphi \longmapsto \varphi|_V,
5013	  \]
5014	  the \(\mathtt{restrictionMap}\), sending a morphism of restricted modules
5015	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to its further restriction \(M|_V \to N|_V\). Concretely, the
5016	  functor \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g : \mathcal{C}/V \to \mathcal{C}/U\) realises
5017	  ``restrict from \(U\) to \(V\)'', and \(\varphi|_V\) is \(\varphi\) pulled back along
5018	  it --- the further restriction of the same morphism of presheaves of modules. This
5019	  map is additive, and it is \emph{semilinear} over the ring restriction
5020	  \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\): for \(f \in R(U)\),
5021	  \[
5022	    (f \cdot \varphi)\big|_V \;=\; R(g)(f) \cdot \bigl(\varphi|_V\bigr).
5023	  \]
5024	  This semilinearity is the compatibility datum that the presheaf assembly of
5025	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} (through \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf}\) /
5026	  \(\mathtt{mk}\)) requires.
5027	\end{lemma}
5028	
5029	\begin{proof}
5030	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
5031	  \leanok
5032	  Further restriction along \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g\) is functorial, so it carries a
5033	  morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to a morphism \(M|_V \to N|_V\) and respects identities and
5034	  composites; in particular the induced map on morphism groups is additive. For the
5035	  semilinearity, recall that the global-scalar action of
5036	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} acts on \(\varphi\) by post-composition with
5037	  the multiplication-by-\(f\) endomorphism \(m_f^{N}\); restricting that endomorphism
5038	  from \(U\) to \(V\) is multiplication by \(f|_V = R(g)(f)\) on \(N|_V\). Since further
5039	  restriction commutes with composition of morphisms, restricting \(f \cdot \varphi =
5040	  m_f^{N} \circ \varphi\) yields \(m_{R(g)(f)}^{N|_V} \circ (\varphi|_V) = R(g)(f) \cdot
5041	  (\varphi|_V)\), which is the asserted compatibility.
5042	\end{proof}

5372	\begin{lemma}
5373	[Restriction commutes with the dual along an open immersion (the C-bridge)]
5374	  \label{lem:dual_restrict_iso}
5375	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_restrict_iso}
5376	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5377	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
5378	  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
5379	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
5380	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5381	  % "Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a morphism
5382	  % of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules.
5383	  % If $\mathcal{F}$ is finitely presented and $f$ is flat,
5384	  % then the canonical map
5385	  % $$
5386	  % f^*\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
5387	  % \longrightarrow
5388	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(f^*\mathcal{F}, f^*\mathcal{G})
5389	  % $$
5390	  % is an isomorphism."
5391	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
5392	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
5393	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
5394	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
5395	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
5396	  \[
5397	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
5398	  \]
5399	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
5400	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
5401	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
5402	\end{lemma}
5403	
5404	\begin{proof}
5405	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5406	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
5407	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
5408	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
5409	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
5410	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
5411	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
5412	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
5413	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
5414	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
5415	
5416	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
5417	  definitional equality: the two sides are not equal on the nose --- they carry
5418	  \emph{different module structures} that a sectionwise ring isomorphism must reconcile
5419	  --- so the comparison must be constructed and its naturality verified, even though the
5420	  underlying additive groups of functionals coincide. The construction lives at the
5421	  \emph{presheaf-of-modules level}, where the residual genuinely sits, and is carried out
5422	  objectwise in the open \(V\) (with \(V \subseteq U\)) from three ingredients.
5423	
5424	  \emph{(a) The per-\(V\) slice equivalence.} Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
5425	  direct-image-of-opens functor \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) is fully faithful with image
5426	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); consequently, for each open \(V \subseteq U\) there is
5427	  an equivalence of slice categories
5428	  \[
5429	    \mathtt{Over}_Y\,V \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Over}_X\bigl(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\,V\bigr),
5430	  \]
5431	  the per-open shadow of the reindexing equivalence
5432	  \(\mathtt{TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence}\). This identifies the indexing slice
5433	  over which the internal hom on \(Y\) computes its value at \(V\) with the corresponding
5434	  slice over \(X\). This is a per-open, presheaf-level instrument, working one open \(V\)
5435	  at a time and respecting the varying-ring \(\mathcal{O}(V)\)-module structure that the
5436	  dual carries; the comparison is built entirely at this presheaf-of-modules level, where
5437	  the residual genuinely lives.
5438	
5439	  \emph{(b) Agreement of the codomain.} The codomain of the internal hom on both sides is
5440	  the structure sheaf. On opens \(V \subseteq U\) the structure sheaf of \(Y\) agrees with
5441	  that of \(X\) under the identification of (a) --- \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V) =
5442	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV)\) up to the canonical comparison --- so the target side of the hom
5443	  matches under the slice reindexing.
5444	
5445	  \emph{(c) The ring-iso transport of the module structure.} The only remaining
5446	  discrepancy is the module structure. The left-hand side
5447	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f\) carries, at \(V\), the \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-module structure;
5448	  the right-hand side \(\mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\) carries the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module
5449	  structure. The sectionwise comparison
5450	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathtt{hom}_V \colon
5451	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}, so
5452	  restriction of scalars along \(\beta_V\) is an \emph{equivalence}
5453	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isEquivalence\_of\_ringEquiv}\)). Its ModuleCat-level shadow
5454	  on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}: it carries the
5455	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
5456	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly.
5457	
5458	  Composing (a), (b) and (c) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
5459	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values, and one checks
5460	  naturality in \(V\). The slice-reindexing coherence (a) is \emph{light}, because
5461	  \(\mathrm{Opens}\) is a thin poset and all the relevant squares commute by
5462	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\); but the module-structure transport (c) does
5463	  \emph{not} trivialise --- it is the substantive content, the place where the two sides
5464	  differ by a real ring isomorphism rather than an identity. Assembling the sectionwise
5465	  isomorphisms into a presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism, and sheafifying through the
5466	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, yields the stated isomorphism
5467	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
5468	\end{proof}

5472	  \label{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
5473	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_isLocallyTrivial}
5474	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso,
5475	        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5476	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
5477	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2)
5478	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213).
5479	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5480	  % "\item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
5481	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
5482	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
5483	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
5484	  % is an isomorphism."
5485	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR,
5486	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item~2).}
5487	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
5488	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
5489	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of
5490	  rank one, i.e.\ \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,L)\).
5491	\end{lemma}
5492	
5493	\begin{proof}
5494	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso,
5495	        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5496	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an affine open \(U\) with a
5497	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\); writing \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) for the
5498	  associated open immersion, it suffices to exhibit an isomorphism
5499	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). This is the following three-step chain:
5500	  \[
5501	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f
5502	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
5503	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U
5504	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_U.
5505	  \]
5506	  \begin{enumerate}
5507	    \item The first isomorphism is the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
5508	      \cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}: restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes
5509	      with the formation of the dual. This is the C-bridge proper; its substantive content
5510	      is the sectionwise ring-iso transport of the module structure
5511	      (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}), as detailed there.
5512	    \item The second isomorphism is the dual applied to the trivialisation
5513	      \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), via the contravariant functoriality of the sheaf-level
5514	      dual in isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\),
5515	      \cref{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}): an isomorphism \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\)
5516	      induces \(\mathtt{dual}(L|_U) \cong \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U\).
5517	    \item The third isomorphism trivialises the dual of the unit,
5518	      \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (\(\mathtt{dual\_unit\_iso}\)):
5519	      the dual of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf itself, the comparison being
5520	      evaluation at \(1 \in \mathcal{O}_U\), under which
5521	      \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U) \cong \mathcal{O}_U\).
5522	  \end{enumerate}
5523	  Composing the three exhibits \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). Hence
5524	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
5525	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).
5526	\end{proof}
5527	
5528	\begin{lemma}
5529	
5530	
5531	\leanok
5532	[Local isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global isomorphism]
5533	  \label{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}
5534	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_restrict}
5535	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi \colon M \to N\) a morphism in
5536	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\).
5537	  Suppose that for every point \(x \in X\) there is an open neighbourhood \(U_x\) of
5538	  \(x\) such that the restriction of \(\varphi\) to \(U_x\) --- the image
5539	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) under the
5540	  restriction functor along the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\colon U_x \to X\) ---
5541	  is an isomorphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_x\). Then \(\varphi\) is an
5542	  isomorphism.
5543	\end{lemma}
5544	
5545	\begin{proof}
5546	
5547	
5548	\leanok
5549	  Being an isomorphism for a morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules can be
5550	  checked on the underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups, and there it can be
5551	  checked stalkwise. Fix a point \(x \in X\). Choose a preimage \(x' \in U_x\) of \(x\)
5552	  under the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\). By hypothesis the restriction
5553	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) is an
5554	  isomorphism, hence so is its image under any functor --- in particular under the
5555	  stalk-at-\(x'\) functor on the underlying ab-sheaves. The natural isomorphism
5556	  ``restriction commutes with stalks'' (\(\mathtt{restrictStalkNatIso}\)) identifies
5557	  this with the stalk of (the underlying ab-sheaf morphism of) \(\varphi\) at the image
5558	  point \(x\); transporting along it shows that stalk is an isomorphism. As this holds
5559	  at every \(x\), and a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups all of whose stalks are
5560	  isomorphisms is itself an isomorphism (stalkwise iso-detection,
5561	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\) on \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\)), the
5562	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism of \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism. Finally the forgetful
5563	  functor \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules.toPresheaf}\) reflects isomorphisms, so \(\varphi\)
5564	  itself is an isomorphism.
5565	\end{proof}
5566	
5567	\begin{definition}
5568	
5569	
5570	
5571	\leanok
5572	[Scheme-level linearity wrapper \(\mathtt{homMk}\) (A-bridge step (ii))]
5573	  \label{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
5574	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homMk}
5575	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Given a morphism
5576	  \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf} \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of
5577	  the underlying presheaves of abelian groups, together with a hypothesis \(hg\) that
5578	  \(g\) is \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear sectionwise, \(\mathtt{homMk}\,g\,hg\) is the
5579	  morphism \(M \to N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) that this data determines. It
5580	  is a thin wrapper that packages \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.homMk}\) at the scheme
5581	  level: the sectionwise-linearity datum promotes the ab-group morphism to a genuine
5582	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Its \(\mathtt{@[simp]}\) companion
5583	  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf\_map\_homMk}\) records that forgetting back recovers \(g\), i.e.\
5584	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,X).\mathtt{map}\,(\mathtt{homMk}\,g\,hg) = g\). This is step
5585	  (ii) of the A-bridge \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}: once the
5586	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism has been glued, \(\mathtt{homMk}\) re-attaches the
5587	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linearity to produce the global morphism of modules.
5588	\end{definition}
5589	
5590	\begin{lemma}
5591	[Compatible local morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global morphism]
5592	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}
5593	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homOfLocalCompat}
5594	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and \(\{U_i\}\) an
5595	  open cover of \(X\). Suppose given, for each \(i\), a morphism
5596	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that
5597	  for all \(i, j\) the restrictions of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree.
5598	  Then there is a unique global morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
5599	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\).
5600	\end{lemma}
5601	
5602	\begin{proof}
5603	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_homMk, lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
5604	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
5605	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
5606	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.
5607	
5608	  \emph{(i) Glue the underlying ab-sheaf morphism.} Forget \(M\) and \(N\) to their
5609	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
5610	  \(\mathcal{H} := \mathtt{presheafHom}\,(M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\,
5611	  (N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
5612	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
5613	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
5614	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
5615	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
5616	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
5617	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
5618	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
5619	  strictly more bookkeeping.
5620	
5621	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}
5622	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,U_i)\)
5623	  manufactured from \(f_i\). Its component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given
5624	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
5625	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
5626	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
5627	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole
5628	  construction is concentrated in the \emph{naturality field} of
5629	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
5630	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
5631	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece that should be
5632	  built and verified first, as a standalone axiom-clean lemma.
5633	
5634	  This \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality is precisely the \emph{section-direction
5635	  slice} of the comparison packaged in \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}:
5636	  converting the local datum \(f_i\), which lives over the open-immersion pullback
5637	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U_i}\), into a section of \(\mathcal{H}\) indexed over the
5638	  slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\) is the one-sided realisation of the open-immersion
5639	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport. As there, the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation is
5640	  controlled by the down-set identity \(\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}(V)) = V\) for
5641	  \(V \subseteq U_i\), and the coherence is automatic on the thin poset
5642	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). This A-engine uses the
5643	  sheaf-site equivalence \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}; the C-bridge
5644	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, by contrast, uses a per-open slice comparison at
5645	  the presheaf level over the \emph{varying} section ring \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), and so
5646	  does \emph{not} reduce to the same sheaf-site root --- the two are independent and
5647	  must be built separately.
5648	
5649	  The remaining four sub-steps are mechanical: (a) the
5650	  cocycle/\(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition on the family
5651	  \((\mathtt{localSection}\,i)_i\), which is exactly the assumed agreement of \(f_i\)
5652	  and \(f_j\) on the overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\); (b) gluing via
5653	  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
5654	  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}
5655	  \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of the underlying presheaves --- the
5656	  \(\top\)-section-to-morphism passage being the global-sections equivalence
5657	  \(\mathtt{presheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) / \(\mathtt{sheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) of the
5658	  hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) (which, since \(\top\) is terminal in \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
5659	  lands the glued section as an honest morphism of presheaves), \emph{not} a hand-unfold
5660	  of the \(\top\)-section; (c) the
5661	  sectionwise linearity check; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
5662	  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).
5663	
5664	  \emph{(ii) Promote to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear.} The linearity equation
5665	  \(g(r \cdot m) = r \cdot g(m)\) is a sectionwise identity between two sections of a
5666	  separated presheaf. It holds on each \(U_i\), because there \(g\) restricts to the
5667	  module morphism \(f_i\); since the two sides agree on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) and the
5668	  ambient presheaf is separated, they agree globally. Packaging \(g\) together with this
5669	  global linearity through the scheme-level wrapper \cref{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
5670	  (\(\mathtt{homMk}\)) yields a morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
5671	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); its restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\) and its
5672	  uniqueness are both inherited from the ab-sheaf amalgamation of step (i).
5673	
5674	  This gluing engine is substantially larger than a routine glue: it is dominated by the
5675	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality field and the convert-and-recover bookkeeping, and
5676	  the naturality is the section-direction slice of
5677	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} --- so its true cost is bounded below by
5678	  building that sheaf-site equivalence, not by the surrounding assembly. It is therefore to be
5679	  scoped as a multi-piece build --- with \(\mathtt{localSection}\), together with its
5680	  naturality, built as a standalone verified lemma first --- rather than a one-shot.
5681	  The step computes no tensor stalk --- it is purely a statement about gluing morphisms
5682	  of sheaves --- and so it does not re-enter the stalk-tensor commutation.
5683	\end{proof}
5684	
5685	\begin{remark}
5686	[How the dual infrastructure discharges the \(\otimes\)-inverse]
5687	  \label{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}
5688	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial,
5689	        lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat, lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict,
5690	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
5691	  Once \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,def:presheaf_dual,%
5692	  lem:internal_hom_isSheaf,lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} are available,
5693	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is discharged with no further missing object:
5694	  take \(L^{-1} := \mathtt{dual}\,L\) (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}), which is a line
5695	  bundle by \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}. The global contraction \(\varepsilon_L
5696	  \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{not} obtained by sheafifying
5697	  the presheaf-level evaluation; it is instead built by \emph{gluing the canonical local
5698	  contraction morphisms}. On each trivialising open \(U\), the
5699	  restriction-compatibility iso \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} carries
5700	  \((L \otimes_X L^{-1})|_U\) to \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U\), and the
5701	  canonical local contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\)
5702	  of the free rank-one module with its dual is the left unitor
5703	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, an isomorphism. These local contractions are canonical,
5704	  hence agree on overlaps, and so glue --- by the A-bridge
5705	  \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat} --- to a single global morphism
5706	  \(\varepsilon_L \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\). Since ``being an
5707	  isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) restricts to an isomorphism on
5708	  each \(U\), it is a global isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim}
5709	  \mathcal{O}_X\) by the B-connector \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}.
5710	
5711	  This descent construction deliberately avoids the forbidden
5712	  ``sheafify-the-evaluation'' route --- which would re-enter the abandoned
5713	  tensor-stalk / sheafification-unit whiskering commutation --- and computes no tensor
5714	  stalk anywhere. It is the realization the ``infrastructure-blocked'' note in
5715	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} points to.
5716	\end{remark}
5717	
5718	\begin{remark}
5719	[Alternative route via object-level descent (fallback)]
5720	  \label{rem:dual_via_stack}
5721	  Should the slice-and-sheafify route of
5722	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} bottom out, the dual can
5723	  instead be assembled by \emph{object-level descent}: glue the local trivial duals
5724	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) along the inverse-transpose transition isomorphisms
5725	  \(g_{ij}^{-\mathsf{T}}\) of \(L\) into a global \(L^{-1}\) on \(X\). Abstractly this
5726	  is effective descent for sheaves of modules on the small Zariski site --- the
5727	  essential-surjectivity of the descent-data functor of an \(\mathtt{IsStack}\)
5728	  pseudofunctor \(U \mapsto (\mathcal{O}_U\text{-modules})\). This is the more
5729	  principled and reusable construction but the larger build (it requires constructing
5730	  the restriction pseudofunctor and proving the stack/effective-descent property),
5731	  and is recorded only as a fallback; the slice internal-hom route above is the
5732	  intended one. It is the same morphism-/object-level descent family on which the
5733	  associator re-route of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} also waits.
5734	\end{remark}

2631	\paragraph{Why the apparent obstruction does not apply.} It is tempting to argue that
2632	the comparison can never be an isomorphism because an (op)lax monoidal functor need
2633	not preserve invertible objects --- global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal, yet
2634	\(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not invertible. But that
2635	counterexample concerns the \emph{lax} tensorator of \emph{pushforward}: \(\Gamma\)
2636	is a right adjoint (global sections), and its comparison
2637	\(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes
2638	\mathcal{G})\) genuinely fails to be an isomorphism. The comparison we need is the
2639	\emph{oplax} \(\delta\) of \emph{pullback}, a \emph{left} adjoint, running the other
2640	way, \(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\). Pullback \emph{provably preserves
2641	local triviality} (\cref{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}): if \(M\) is trivialised
2642	by \(\mathcal{O}_U\) on a cover \(\{U_i\}\), then \(f^*M\) is trivialised by
2643	\(\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}U_i}\) on \(\{f^{-1}U_i\}\) --- using only
2644	\(f^*\mathcal{O} \cong \mathcal{O}\) and the compatibility of pullback with
2645	restriction along open immersions. Consequently, on locally-trivial objects, both
2646	sides of \(\delta\) are locally trivial, and \(\delta\) restricts to a comparison
2647	between the structure sheaf and itself on each chart, where it is forced to be an
2648	isomorphism. The bare oplax structure supplies \(\delta\) as a \emph{morphism} for
2649	free (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}); the entire remaining content is the
2650	chart-local check that, on a trivialising cover, \(\delta\) is an isomorphism.

6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
