1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.a sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem, the technical heart that
8	% turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
9	% morphism \(X \to S\) with coherent input \(\F\) into a finite collection of
10	% locally-closed strata over which \(\F\) becomes flat. Mathlib (master
11	% \texttt{b80f227}) has neither a coherent-sheaf flatness predicate over a
12	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter
13	% is the prover-ready specification for the new file
14	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean} which a
15	% subsequent iter (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
16	%
17	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
18	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
19	
20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the

9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary

12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved

27	%
28	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is

36	% \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} (the A.4.b sub-row). The chapter
37	% records the dependency but does not re-prove it.

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The

4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting

13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      \(=\) localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
29	% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\) alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
72

1	\chapter{Rigidity over a base field \(k\): morphisms from a genus-\(0\) curve to a group scheme are constant}
2	\label{chap:RigidityKbar}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityKbar.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157): The project's COMMITTED genus-0 rigidity route is now route
6	% (c) --- the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity-lemma stack developed in its own
7	% chapter \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (Lean file
8	% AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, upstream of Jacobian.lean). The headline
9	% there, thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV (char-free, [IsAlgClosed k-bar] only), is what
10	% genusZeroWitness consumes. THIS chapter now documents the over-k-bar declaration
11	% rigidity_over_kbar (which carries [CharZero k-bar]) together with the FALLBACK route (a):
12	% the df=0 / cotangent-triviality + Serre-duality decomposition and the closed, axiom-clean
13	% chart-algebra envelope (which supplies only the converse "df=0 implies constant"). Route
14	% (a) is retained in tree, off the critical path; the df=0 framing below is the fallback,
15	% NOT the committed route.
16	
17	This chapter introduces the project's named declaration for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-\(0\) Albanese-witness argument: rigidity of morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, \textbf{over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero} (the alg-closed pivot of \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot" below; descent of the constancy conclusion to a general base field \(k\) is a separate downstream step, hosted in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). The classical statement (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4) is
18	
19	\begin{quote}
20	  \emph{Let \(A\) be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). Then every morphism \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant.}
21	\end{quote}
22	
23	This is the keystone classical input for the C.2.d sub-step of Jacobian.tex \S C.2: the genus-\(0\) universal-property step of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} that concludes the universal-pointed-morphism factorisation by ruling out positive-dimensional images of \(f \colon C \to A\) when \(C\) has genus \(0\) and a \(k\)-rational marked point.
24	
25	\paragraph{Disposition (iter-155): \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} is a gated NAMED GAP.} The named declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} is landed with a \texttt{sorry} body and is held as a \textbf{named gap} in the same family as \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Jacobian.tex) and the deferred Serre-duality / Riemann--Roch stack --- honestly disclosed, not on a prover lane. The substantive content (the C.2.d sub-step below, ruling out a \(1\)-dimensional image of \(f\)) reduces to the vanishing of the cotangent pull-back \(\mathrm df \colon f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{C/\bar k}\), and a decisive iter-155 \texttt{mathlib-analogist} consult (\texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}) established that \textbf{producing \(\mathrm df = 0\) is irreducibly a global-sections fact}, gated on two genuinely-absent inputs:
26	
27	\begin{itemize}
28	  \item \textbf{(gap i) Global cotangent-triviality of \(A\):} \(\Omega_{A/\bar k} \cong \mathcal O_A^{\oplus g}\), generated by translation-invariant differentials. The presheaf globalisation that would build this (\texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) was \textbf{excised from the Lean tree at iter-145}; \texttt{GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} \(+\) \texttt{shearMulRight} survive only as a \emph{design template}. Estimated \(\sim 800\)--\(1500\) LOC. Mathlib's only ``group cotangent is trivial'' idiom is the smooth-manifold \texttt{GroupLieAlgebra} stack, which does not port to schemes.
29	  \item \textbf{(gap ii) Serre duality \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\)} for the genus-\(0\) curve. Given the global trivialisation of gap (i), \(\mathrm df \in H^0\bigl(C, \mathcal{H}om(f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr) \cong \bigl(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr)^{\oplus g}\), so \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\). Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has no dualizing sheaf and no Riemann--Roch; the project's \texttt{Cohomology/*} stack computes \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (genus) but has \textbf{no \(H^0\)-vanishing lemma and no genus\(\leftrightarrow\Omega\) bridge}. The genus-\(0\) hypothesis \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) does \emph{not} yield \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) without the duality. Estimated \(\sim 3000\)--\(8000\) LOC.
30	\end{itemize}
31	
32	\noindent The chart-algebra envelope of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM), \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}, and the delegate \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}), which \emph{is} closed and axiom-clean, supplies only the \textbf{converse} half of the bridge: ``if \(\mathrm df = 0\) then \(f\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) (is constant)''. It \emph{cannot} produce \(\mathrm df = 0\): on each affine chart \(V \subseteq C\), \(\Omega_{C/\bar k}(V)\) is free of rank \(1\) (nonzero), so a chart-by-chart computation never detects the vanishing --- the vanishing materialises only on global sections. Earlier framings in this chapter (the piece-(iv) ``DEFERRED, not on the C.2.d critical path'' disposition and the ``No-Serre-duality layer'') asserted that the chart-algebra route \emph{avoids} Serre duality; \textbf{that is false}, and is corrected throughout \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) is \emph{on} the critical path for producing \(\mathrm df = 0\), not avoided.
33	
34	The project does \textbf{not} open a ``produce \(\mathrm df = 0\)'' prover lane until one of two candidate closure routes is built; the decision between them is a leading open strategic question and the chapter commits to neither:
35	\begin{itemize}
36	  \item \textbf{Route (a):} build gaps (i) \(+\) (ii) directly.
37	  \item \textbf{Route (b):} the dual-abelian-variety keystone --- ``morphisms \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) are constant because \(\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0\)''. Earlier rejected as ``needs \(\Pic^0\)'', but since the positive-genus arm of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Route A) needs the \(\Pic^0\) engine \emph{regardless}, route (b) may amortise the shared machinery and be globally cheaper than a standalone Serre-duality build.
38	\end{itemize}
39	
40	\paragraph{Iter-152 alg-closed pivot.} The project committed an architectural pivot: rigidity is proved \textbf{only over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero}, and the constancy conclusion descends to a general base field \(k\) downstream. The reason is that the chart-algebra core lemma \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM) is mathematically \emph{false} as a bare \(B\)-only algebra lemma: a prover lane exhibited two counterexamples that satisfy every hypothesis yet have \(\ker D \supsetneq \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap})\) --- namely \(B = k \times k\) (finite \'etale over \(k\)) and \(B = \mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)\) over \(\mathbb Q\). The missing content is precisely ``\(k\) algebraically closed in \(B\)''. Adding the \emph{joint} hypotheses \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[IsDomain B]} makes it true: \texttt{[IsDomain B]} kills the \(B = k \times k\) counterexample and \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} kills the \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)/\mathbb Q\) counterexample. (Neither hypothesis suffices alone; the iter-151 note that ``\texttt{[IsDomain B]} alone fails'' considered the hypotheses only one-at-a-time.) Over an algebraically closed base the \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} constants lemma also collapses to a one-liner via \texttt{IsAlgClosed.algebraMap\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}, and the entire (S3.\textsc{sep}/\textsc{pi}) base-change-to-\(\bar k\) chain is descoped from the M2.a critical path.
41	
42	\textbf{The Lean declaration's signature now carries \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[CharZero kbar]}} (matching the theorem's existing docstring, which already described \(\bar k\) as algebraically closed --- the hypothesis was simply absent from the formal statement). Prior strategy iterations had instead routed through base-change to \(\bar k\) followed by Galois descent of morphism equality (M2.c, \(\sim 4\)--\(8\) iter / \(300\)--\(500\) LOC); the iter-127 framing then claimed all three pieces of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile build over an \emph{arbitrary} base field, dropping descent entirely. The alg-closed pivot supersedes that framing for the rigidity theorem itself: rigidity is established over \(\bar k\), and the descent of the constancy conclusion to a general \(k\) is a short downstream consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective} (faithfully-flat covers are epimorphisms of schemes), with the detailed descent living in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter. The group-scheme cotangent-triviality pieces (i)/(iii) below remain genuinely intrinsic to the group scheme and are stated over a general base where that is sound; only the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constancy chain (the KDM/constants core) requires the alg-closed base.
43	
44	\section{Statement}
45	
46	% NOTE: classical rigidity for morphisms from \(\mathbb P^1\) (or genus-0
47	% pointed proper curves) to abelian / group varieties; standard
48	% references are Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch.~II \S4 (rational
49	% curves on abelian varieties; paywalled, not bundled) and Hartshorne,
50	% *Algebraic Geometry*, Ex.~IV.4 (not bundled in references/). The
51	% cotangent-route closure used here ties into Stacks Tag 04QM (smooth
52	% over fields ⇒ geometrically reduced; references/stacks-varieties.tex,
53	% lemma-smooth-geometrically-normal) for the chart-level differential
54	% machinery.
55	\begin{theorem}
56	  \label{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar}
58	  \leanok
59	  % SOURCE: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch. II §4 (verbatim text not yet
60	  % retrieved — paywalled, no open copy in references/). No SOURCE QUOTE
61	  % is supplied: the source is not bundled and recalled text must not be
62	  % substituted (citation discipline).
63	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, II §4.}
64	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field of characteristic zero (Lean: \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[CharZero kbar]}; in the use case, the algebraic closure of a base field \(k\), with descent of the conclusion to \(k\) handled downstream in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). These two hypotheses match the theorem's existing docstring and are required by the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constancy chain (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero}, \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}); see \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot". Let \(C : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(\genus(C) = 0\) (i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\)). Let \(A : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(\bar k\), with identity element \(\eta_A \in A(\bar k)\). Let
65	  \[
66	    f \colon C \longrightarrow A
67	  \]
68	  be a morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes over \(\Spec \bar k\), and let \(p \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point such that \(f(p) = \eta_A\). Then \(f\) equals the constant morphism at \(\eta_A\):
69	  \[
70	    f \;=\; \bigl(C \to \Spec \bar k \xrightarrow{\,\eta_A\,} A\bigr).
71	  \]
72	
73	  \emph{Encoding note (iter-126).} The Lean signature uses the abstract characterization \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathtt{IsProper}\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \genus(C) = 0\) instead of a literal \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) scheme. The two encodings are equivalent over \(\bar k\) once the curve has a \(\bar k\)-rational point (Brauer--Severi triviality + Riemann--Roch on \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)); the abstract form is used because Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} does not ship a packaged \(\mathtt{ProjectiveSpace}\,n\,S\) as a \(\mathtt{Scheme.Over}\,S\), and the C.2.b–C.2.d proof decomposition below works for any smooth proper geometrically integral genus-0 curve over \(\bar k\) without explicit \(\mathbb P^1\) API.
74	\end{theorem}
75	
76	\begin{remark}
77	  \label{rem:rigidity_over_kbar_dim_dichotomy}
78	  
79	  The conclusion is vacuously true when \(A\) is zero-dimensional: in that case \(A = \Spec \bar k\) and every morphism \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) trivially. The substantive content of the theorem is the positive-dimensional case, which decomposes into the steps of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_proof_decomposition}.
80	\end{remark}

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
45	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.
60	\end{itemize}
61	
62	The remaining Mathlib gap for the \emph{theorem} (as opposed to the \emph{definition}) of genus:
63	
64	\begin{itemize}
65	  \item \textbf{Serre finiteness for \(H^i(C, \mathcal F)\) on a proper \(k\)-curve.} Mathlib's \(\ModuleCat.\mathtt{finite\_ext}\) provides \(\Module.\mathtt{Finite}\) for module-Ext only, not sheaf-Ext. The Čech-cohomology scaffold is the recommended direction.
66	\end{itemize}
67	
68	\section{User authorisation of \texttt{noncomputable}}
69	\label{sec:Genus_noncomputable}
70	
71	The original challenge file writes \(\mathtt{def\ genus}\), not \(\mathtt{noncomputable\ def\ genus}\). Because \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\), \(\Abelian.\Ext.\mathtt{instModule}\), and the structure-sheaf-as-\(k\)-module construction are all noncomputable in Mathlib, the honest closure of the body requires the \(\mathtt{noncomputable}\) modifier on the \(\mathtt{def}\). The user explicitly authorised agents to add this modifier as a non-signature change: names, argument types, and argument order remain verbatim.
72	
73	\section{What this iteration does and does not}
74	
75	This iteration \emph{defines} the genus. It does not \emph{compute} it on any specific curve, and it does not prove that the result equals the geometric genus in the classical sense (which requires Serre finiteness for non-vacuity and Riemann--Roch for verification on examples). The downstream theorem \(\mathtt{smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) in \(\mathtt{Jacobian.lean}\) states that the Jacobian of \(C\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\) --- that theorem will require Serre finiteness on the way and is queued for the iteration that closes the Jacobian construction itself.
76

1	\chapter{The relative cotangent presheaf}
2	\label{chap:differentials}
3	
4	This chapter develops the relative cotangent \emph{presheaf} \(\Omega_{X/S}\) of a morphism of schemes \(f \colon X \to S\), starting from the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) already available in Mathlib. The output is a presheaf of \(\struct{X}\)-modules whose sections on each open \(V \subseteq X\) are the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)\) induced by \(f\), where \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\) denotes the \emph{presheaf-level} inverse-image functor (left Kan extension along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\)).
5	
6	The downstream role of this chapter is to provide the \emph{forward direction} of the standard smoothness criterion: smoothness of \(f\) implies that on every affine chart \(V \to U\) (with \(V \subseteq X\), \(U \subseteq S\) affine and \(f V \subseteq U\)) the appLE-algebra K\"ahler differential \(\Omega_{\Gamma(X, V)\,/\,\Gamma(S, U)}\) is a free \(\Gamma(X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\). This is the Jacobian-criterion direction and is what connects to the dimension hypothesis on the Jacobian: the smoothness of the curve \(C \to \Spec k\) in the Jacobian definition is the special case \(n = 1\), and the relative dimension of \(\Jac(C)\) is read off from the rank via the same forward implication. The bridge between this affine-chart-wise algebra-K\"ahler form and the section module of the relative cotangent presheaf \(\Omega_{X/S}\) was the former content of milestone~M1; the bridge declaration was excised iter-126 (zero in-tree consumers) and replaced in this chapter by the standalone K\"ahler-localization utilities of Section~REF, the off-loop Mathlib-PR work of which proceeds from \texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md}. The converse implication of the forward smoothness criterion is \emph{mathematically false} as stated and is documented separately in Section~REF.
7	
8	\section{The relative cotangent presheaf}
9	
10	\begin{definition}
11	
12	\leanok
13	[Relative cotangent presheaf of a morphism]
14	  \label{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
15	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}
16	  Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes. The presheaf-level inverse-image presheaf of rings \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S}\) on \(X\) (the left Kan extension of \(\struct{S}\) along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\), given on each open \(V \subseteq X\) by the colimit \(\mathrm{colim}_{W : \,f V \subseteq W \subseteq S}\,\struct{S}(W)\)) is paired with \(\struct{X}\) by the canonical adjunction-transpose \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S} \to \struct{X}\) of the comorphism \(f^{\sharp}\colon\struct{S}\to f_{*}\struct{X}\). Applying the presheaf-of-modules K\"ahler differential construction \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\) to this map of presheaves of commutative rings produces a presheaf of \(\struct{X}\)-modules on \(X\), the \emph{relative cotangent presheaf} \(\Omega_{X/S}\).
17	\end{definition}
18	
19	\begin{lemma}
20	
21	\leanok
22	[Sections of \(\Omega_{X/S}\) on an open]
23	  \label{lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}
24	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler}
25	  
26	  For every open \(V \subseteq X\), the sections of the relative cotangent presheaf are, by construction, the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)\) induced by \(f\):
27	  \[
28	    \Omega_{X/S}(V) \;=\; \Omega_{\struct{X}(V) \,/\, (f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)}.
29	  \]
30	  Here \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) = \mathrm{colim}_{W : \,f V \subseteq W \subseteq S}\,\struct{S}(W)\) is the presheaf-level inverse-image section ring, \emph{not} \(\struct{S}(U)\) for an arbitrary affine open \(U \supseteq f V\). The relationship between this colimit-source K\"ahler module and the classical ring-theoretic \(\Omega_{B/A}\) for \(A = \struct{S}(U)\), \(B = \struct{X}(V)\) on an affine chart \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}U\) with \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) affine is the content of the bridge documented in Section~REF.
31	\end{lemma}
32	
33	\begin{proof}
34	  
35	  \leanok
36	  By definition of \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\): the construction is built object-wise to be the Mathlib \texttt{KaehlerDifferential} module of the ring map at each open. The identification is therefore by \texttt{rfl} after unfolding the definition. The colimit description of \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)\) is the definition of \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.pullback} as the left Kan extension along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\) in Mathlib.
37	\end{proof}
38	
39	\begin{remark}[Comparison with Mathlib's ring-level \(\Omega_{B/A}\)]
40	  \label{rem:kahler_compatibility}
41	  Mathlib provides the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) together with the universal derivation \(\mathrm{d} \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}\). The construction of \(\Omega_{X/S}\) on each open is, by definition, this Mathlib module applied at the ring level of the presheaf, so all ring-level Mathlib lemmas about \(\Omega_{B/A}\) transfer verbatim under Lemma~REF — but for the \emph{colimit-source} ring \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)\), not for \(\struct{S}(U)\). Transferring to the classical \(A = \struct{S}(U)\) source on an affine chart is the bridge of Section~REF.
42	\end{remark}
43	
44	\section{Smoothness criterion for \(\Omega_{X/S}\) (forward direction)}
45	
46	The forward direction of the textbook smoothness criterion identifies a necessary condition on the relative K\"ahler differentials: every point of \(X\) admits an affine chart \(V \to U\) over which the appLE-algebra K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) is free of the prescribed rank, with \(A = \struct{S}(U)\) and \(B = \struct{X}(V)\). This is the global version of the algebraic-side fact that an \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~algebra has a free K\"ahler differential module of rank \(n\). We state and prove the theorem directly on the appLE-algebra K\"ahler module, sidestepping the presheaf bridge of Section~REF. The converse implication (a local-freeness hypothesis on \(\Omega\) alone implying smoothness) is \emph{false}; see Section~REF.
47	
48	\begin{theorem}\leanok
49	[Smooth implies \(\Omega\) locally free (algebra-K\"ahler form)]
50	  \label{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth_locally_free_omega}
52	  
53	  Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). If \(f\) is smooth of relative dimension \(n\), then for every point \(x \in X\) there exist an affine open \(U \subseteq S\), an affine open \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\), such that the K\"ahler differential module
54	  \[
55	    \Omega_{\Gamma(X, V) \,/\, \Gamma(S, U)}
56	  \]
57	  (over the appLE algebra structure \(\Gamma(S, U) \to \Gamma(X, V)\) induced by \(f\)) is a free \(\Gamma(X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\).
58	\end{theorem}
59	
60	\begin{proof}
61	  
62	  \leanok
63	  Fix \(x \in X\). We use the abbreviation \(A := \Gamma(S, U)\) and \(B := \Gamma(X, V)\) for the section rings on the affine chart we will produce; the algebra map \(A \to B\) is the appLE map \((f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}\) induced by \(f\).
64	
65	  \emph{Step 1: extract a standard-smooth chart at \(x\).} The auto-generated \texttt{mk\_iff} bridge
66	  \[
67	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} \quad [\text{verified}]
68	  \]
69	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Smooth}; produced by the \texttt{@[mk\_iff]} attribute on the class \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}) unfolds the hypothesis \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(f\) into: for every \(x \in X\), there exist affine opens \(U \subseteq S\) and \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \leq f^{-1}U\), such that the induced algebra map \((f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}\colon A \to B\) satisfies \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(A\)~\(B\).
70	
71	  \emph{Step 2: pass to plain standard-smooth.} The lemma
72	  \[
73	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth} \quad [\text{verified}]
74	  \]
75	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmooth}) yields \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth}~\(A\)~\(B\) from the relative-dimension-tagged form, unlocking the cotangent-module machinery in the next two steps.
76	
77	  \emph{Step 3: free K\"ahler module on the chart.} The Mathlib instance
78	  \[
79	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
80	  \]

3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Cross01Substrate.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-189 plan-phase). This chapter is a project-bespoke

23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:cross01_substrate_setup}
25	
26	The Lane B cocycle \texttt{gmScalingP1\_chart\_agreement\_cross01} reduces, after
27	the iter-188 (III.c) separated-locus structural setup, to a single substantive
28	factorisation: a pair morphism
29	\(s_{\text{pair}} : T \to T_0 \times_X T_1\) into a fibre product over the
30	projective line bar \(\mathrm{PLB}_{\overline{k}}\) must factor through the
31	diagonal closed immersion
32	\(\Delta : T_0 \times_{X} T_1 \hookrightarrow T_0 \times_{\overline{k}} T_1\)
33	of the separated-over-\(\overline{k}\) target. Concretely the missing primitive is
34	the equivalence
35	\[
36	  \exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g \iff \mathrm{range}(g) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i)
37	\]
38	for a closed immersion \(i\) with reduced source/target hypothesis, which Mathlib
39	ships only in the kernel-inequality form (\(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\),
40	keyed on \(f.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)). Substrate~1 fills this gap.
41	
42	The same Lane B cocycle, together with the two off-target instances
43	\texttt{gm\_geomIrred} and \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}, also requires
44	\[

46	\]
47	for a homogeneous generator \(f\) of positive degree in the
48	\(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading. Substrate~2 supplies this via a
49	canonical iso chain

52	  \;\cong\; \mathrm{MvPolynomial}_{\mathrm{Unit}}(\mathrm{Away}\,f)
53	\]
54	followed by localisation at \(X()\), reducing the claim to the
55	already-formalised pattern from \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}.

1	\chapter{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology with \(k\)-module coefficients}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}
3	
4	This chapter constructs the Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology valued in \(k\)-modules, and develops the \v{C}ech-acyclicity machinery for affine basic-open covers. Together these provide the categorical engine for the Serre finiteness argument (Phase~A step~6). The carriers \(H^n(X,\mathcal F)\) and \(H^{\prime n}(U,\mathcal F)\) live in Chapter~REF and are imported here.
5	
6	The chapter introduces no protected declarations: every definition, lemma, and instance here is auxiliary, supporting the construction of \(H^1(C,\mathcal O_C)\) in Definition~REF.
7	
8	\section{Functoriality of cohomology in the open variable}
9	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
10	
11	The cohomology-of-an-open carrier \(H^{\prime n}(X,\mathcal F)\) is contravariantly functorial in the open variable \(X\). Assembling this functoriality yields a presheaf
12	\[
13	  X \longmapsto H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F),
14	\]
15	and, varying \(\mathcal F\) as well, a bifunctor from sheaves of \(k\)-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. The codomain is the category of abelian groups because \(\Ext\) is always valued there, regardless of the linear enrichment of the source category; the per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is inherited from the reducible wrapper on \(H^{\prime n}\).
16	
17	\begin{definition}\leanok
18	[Cohomology presheaf functor, \(H'\) flavor]
19	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
20	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
21	  
22	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a category equipped with a Grothendieck topology \(J\), and assume sheafification and \(\Ext\) exist for sheaves of \(k\)-modules on \(J\). For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf functor
23	  \[
24	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(k, J) \;:\; \Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k) \longrightarrow (C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat)
25	  \]
26	  by the standard composition of the Yoneda embedding, the free-\(k\)-module functor, sheafification, and the \(n\)-th \(\Ext\) functor.
27	\end{definition}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  
31	  \leanok
32	  The body is a direct categorical composition mirroring the general sheaf-cohomology presheaf functor. Universe pinning is required because the morphism universe of the sheaf category lives one level above the object universe.
33	\end{proof}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Cohomology presheaf, \(H'\) flavor]
37	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf}
39	  
40	  In the setting of Definition~REF, let \(\mathcal F\) be a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf
41	  \[
42	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F) \;:\; C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat
43	  \]
44	  to be the evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
45	\end{definition}
46	
47	\begin{proof}
48	  
49	  \leanok
50	  Direct evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
51	\end{proof}
52	
53	\begin{remark}
54	  For every object \(X\) of \(C\), the underlying type of \(\mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F)(X)\) is definitionally equal to \(H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F)\). The per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is therefore inherited through the \(H'\) wrapper.
55	\end{remark}
56	
57	\begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
58	  The cohomology presheaf functor is declared as a non-computable definition and the presheaf as a non-computable abbrev, matching the reducibility conventions of the general sheaf-cohomology API. The abbrev form is load-bearing: downstream definitional unfolding needs to see through to the fiber-level \(H'\) carrier.
59	\end{remark}
60	
61	\section{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence}
62	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_mayer_vietoris}
63	
64	A Mayer--Vietoris square for a Grothendieck topology \(J\) on a category \(C\) is a pullback square
65	% NOTE: rendered as a 2x2 array for KaTeX (leanblueprint web); the PDF
66	% build renders the same data via tikz-cd if the package is available.
67	\[
68	  \begin{array}{ccc}
69	    X_1 & \longrightarrow & X_2 \\
70	    \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
71	    X_3 & \longrightarrow & X_4
72	  \end{array}
73	\]
74	that is a \(J\)-cover: the pair of morphisms \(X_2 \to X_4\) and \(X_3 \to X_4\) is a covering family, and \(X_1\) is the pullback. For a sheaf \(\mathcal F\), the cohomology groups of the four corners are linked by a long exact sequence whose building blocks are restriction maps, biproduct maps, and a connecting homomorphism.
75	
76	\subsection{The restriction maps}
77

79	[Sum of two restriction maps]
80	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_toBiprod}

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
5	% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
6	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
7	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
8	
9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
37	
38	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
39	
40	\begin{definition}\leanok
41	[Higher direct image]
42	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
44	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
45	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
46	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
47	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
48	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
49	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
50	  % The file is not yet imported by the aggregator (orphan module).
51	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
52	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
53	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
54	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
55	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
57	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
58	  % the sheaf
59	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
60	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
61	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
62	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
63	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
64	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
65	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
66	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
67	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
68	  sheafification of the presheaf
69	  \[
70	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
71	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
72	  \]
73	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
74	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
75	\end{definition}

7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and

41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote

52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and

55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)

59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit

64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}

3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68

2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80
